source.summary: Crucible Health: inference latency spike + transient 5xx on /v1/generate (INC-9821)
source.description:
Customer escalation relayed by Stephanie Nguyen. Crucible Health reported sudden latency spike and burst of 5xx on inference endpoint /v1/generate beginning ~2026-08-28 07:55 PDT. Reported impact: median latency increased from ~120ms to ~900ms; throughput dropped from ~250 req/s to ~150 req/s for Crucible production key; initial customer-observed 5xx rate ~8% during the window; multiple batches timed out and downstream throttles triggered. Customer had a critical demo at 10:00 PDT.

Immediate hypotheses (serving): autoscaler oscillation under burst + degraded single shard causing head-of-line blocking.

Immediate mitigations executed: emergency scale-up of dedicated pool (target +20% capacity) + temporary concurrency limit increase + drain suspected bad instance(s) + enable routing of new requests to compatible fallback model variant (lower context) to reduce per-token work.

Outcome (updated after investigation): improvements observed within ~9–12 minutes; throughput returned to baseline and tail latency normalized after draining the degraded node(s) and reverting autoscaler policy.

Updated root cause (post-incident): the triggering event was an autoscaler policy update that reduced stabilization/cooldown and interacted poorly with a burst in queue depth, causing scale oscillation. One GPU instance concurrently entered a degraded driver state (intermittent kernel launch stalls rather than a hard OOM), amplifying tail latency and increasing request timeouts which surfaced as 5xx at the gateway. The instance replacement lag plus oscillation extended the incident duration.

Impact (finalized): peak 5xx rate revised to ~6–8% (varied by minute) with ~10–12 minute period of elevated errors; throughput dipped to ~60% of baseline at trough. No evidence of data integrity or security issues.
comments: 2026-08-28 (Stephanie Nguyen): Forwarded urgent report from Crucible. Asks: infra/serving triage immediately; CS to own comms + schedule exec sync 09:00 PDT; finance to pre-approve priority burst capacity if needed. Customer attachments: request_logs_20260828.zip, detailed_kpi_snapshot.csv.

2026-08-28 (Marcus Lin): Initial hypotheses: autoscaler oscillation under sudden burst + failing shard causing head-of-line blocking. Immediate actions: emergency scale dedicated pool (+20%), temporarily increase max concurrency, route new requests to secondary model variant (lower context), isolate/drain problematic instance(s). ETA for measurable improvement: ~8–12 minutes after scale. Will update INC-9821.

2026-08-28 (Hannah Schmitt): On comms. Proposed external update: mitigation in flight; expect improvement within ~10 minutes; reconvene 09:00 PDT. Requested temporary SLA exception window + demo support. Asked finance to greenlight burst billing pending postmortem.

2026-08-28 (Stephanie Nguyen -> customer): Sent external update: emergency scaling, drain suspected degraded instance, fallback routing enabled. Set expectation of measurable improvement in 8–12 minutes and 09:00 PDT exec sync.

2026-08-29 (Marcus Lin): Investigation summary shared internally/with customer on thread: reverted autoscaler policy update; added cooldown to prevent oscillation; drained/reprovisioned affected instance; hotfix to prevent recurrence. Impact noted as ~8% peak 5xx and ~10 minute window. Action items: customer postmortem by EOD; harden autoscaler testing + canary for autoscaler changes; product routing knob for rapid cross-model fallback; finance confirm priority-burst billing terms.

2026-09-01 (Marcus Lin): Correction after deeper node telemetry review: primary node issue was intermittent driver/kernel launch stalls (no sustained OOM). Updated remediation: rolled new base image + added driver health-check gate to remove degraded nodes faster; autoscaler change now includes 30m canary + enforced cooldown floor. Linked PRs: serving-runtime#18422 (health gate + faster eviction), infra-autoscaler#771 (cooldown floor + canary checklist).

2026-09-03 (Stephanie Nguyen): Closed internal ticket after customer postmortem delivered and follow-up technical review scheduled for next week. Finance confirmed no additional burst-capacity charges for incident window; goodwill credit handled via CS.

source.summary: SEV-2: eu-west latency spike after runtime build 2026.03.18.0 (kernel selection)
source.description:
Issue summary: p95 latency regression for long-context traffic in eu-west after runtime build 2026.03.18.0.

Impact: SLO burn for chat long-context bucket; customers saw slower responses but low error rate.

Environment: prod eu-west, A100 pools.

Investigation notes: Suspected kernel selection heuristic change; perf-canary shows regression aligned with build.

Mitigation: Disable heuristic via feature flag; force kernel to prior stable variant.
comments: Ethan Park: Seeing p95 2x for long-context bucket in eu-west after 2026.03.18.0. Starting rollback via feature flag.
Priya Natarajan: Confirmed. Kernel-autotuner selecting variant 'fused_attn_v3' for mixed seq lengths; looks wrong.
David Kim: Any reason this is eu-west only?
Ethan Park: eu-west pools have different seq length distro and A100 driver minor version; likely interacting with heuristic.
Priya Natarajan: Flag disabled; metrics improving. Will write postmortem and add canary gate for eu-west long-context.
source.resolution: Resolved by disabling new kernel selection heuristic via feature flag and documenting rollback. Follow-ups tracked in postmortem INC-2026-0318.

source.summary: Device attestation key rotation breaks CLI session binding for internal devtools
source.description:
Issue summary:
A recent rotation of the device attestation signing key (identity-service) appears to have invalidated existing CLI session bindings. Developer CLI clients receive repeated MFA prompts and then a hard 'session mismatch' error that prevents access to multiple internal tools (devtools-cli, runbook-runner, internal-grafana-proxy).

Impact:
- ~45 engineers unable to run infra CLI commands or access runbook tooling from corporate devices.
- Automated runbooks triggered from CI intermittently fail due to inability to exchange ephemeral session tokens.
- No broad user-facing customer impact, but critical for oncall and incident response workflows.

Environment:
- Prod control plane (identity-service) in us-west-2.
- Affected clients primarily macOS and Linux corporate laptops with device-attestation enabled.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Attempt to login with devtools-cli from a corporate laptop with device attestation enabled.
2) Complete MFA challenge when prompted.
3) Observe repeated MFA prompt cycles followed by: 'ERROR: session binding mismatch: attestation-signature-verified=false' and 401 from cli-proxy.

Expected behavior:
- Attestation signing key rotation should be seamless: new key should be trusted by the control plane and clients bound to prior attestation should either be re-chained automatically or receive a clear recovery prompt.

Actual behavior:
- Clients loop on MFA then fail with session binding mismatch. Manual logout and login sometimes works after several attempts but not consistently.

Logs / error excerpts:
- cli-proxy: "WARN session_verifier: signature_check_failed key_id=attest-2026-03-10 reason=unknown_format"
- identity-service: "ERROR attestation: signature-validation failed for device=host-2345 status=untrusted-key"
- devtools-cli client: "session binding mismatch (attestation-signature-verified=false)
  token exchange failed: 401"

Temporary workaround:
- Some engineers can bypass device-attestation by using a personal device (not allowed by policy for many oncall flows).
- Support can issue a one-time session token via internal support console for immediate access (manual, rate limited).

Next steps requested / acceptance criteria:
- Determine whether new signing key was rolled out without updating the control plane truststore or if clients are sending an old key reference.
- Reproduce on staging and identify minimal rollback or hotfix path that restores session binding for corporate devices.
- Provide mitigation plan that restores oncall access within 4 hours or an agreed mitigation that allows runbook-triggering from CI.
steps_to_reproduce: Install devtools-cli v2.3.1 on a corporate macOS laptop with device attestation enabled
Run 'devtools login' and follow prompts
Complete MFA when prompted until the client fails with session binding mismatch
logs: [cli-proxy] WARN session_verifier: signature_check_failed key_id=attest-2026-03-10 reason=unknown_format
[identity-service] ERROR attestation: signature-validation failed for device=host-2345 status=untrusted-key
[devtools-cli] ERROR session binding mismatch: attestation-signature-verified=false
comments: 2026-03-12 10:22 - Ava Chen (reporter): Filing after oncall reported inability to run emergency runbooks; attaching initial logs.
2026-03-12 11:05 - Luis Martinez (assignee): Acknowledged. Looks like key rotation completed last night per deploy; triaging with identity team now. Assigning to SRE for immediate investigation.
2026-03-12 12:17 - Maya Patel (SRE): Confirmed rotation of attestation key id=attest-2026-03-10 at 02:00 UTC. Control plane appears to have an extra unknown format entry in truststore on one replica.

2026-03-13 03:42 - Luis Martinez: Rolled temporary fix on one identity-service replica to accept legacy-format keys; partial recovery observed (approx 60% of affected clients regained access). Investigating why replica parity broke.
2026-03-14 09:10 - Security (note): Need approval for any rollback of key rotation; rollback decision requires cataloging keys and audit records. Security has been looped in.
2026-03-14 10:40 - Luis Martinez: Next action - coordinate controlled rollback or truststore reconciliation during off-peak window. Preparing runbook and communication for oncall.
investigation_notes: Identity team deploy log shows key rotation job succeeded but truststore config applied to cluster via templated configmap may have had a YAML encoding mismatch (base64 vs raw).
Metrics show spike in 401s from cli-proxy at 02:05 UTC, aligning with the deploy window.
Partial hotfix accepted legacy keys on one replica only; suggests config propagation or bootstrap ordering issue during rotation.
No evidence of compromised keys; rotation initiated per schedule. Security audit required before any full rollback.
Hypothesis: templated configmap failing to encode key metadata correctly for a subset of replicas leading to signature parsing errors.
resolution_notes: Workaround in place: manual one-time session tokens and temporary legacy-key acceptance on limited replicas to restore oncall workflows.
Planned next steps: coordinate truststore reconciliation patch (small config fix) and gradual rollout to replicas with monitoring. Schedule window: 2026-03-15 04:00-05:00 UTC.
Follow-ups: add end-to-end integration test for key rotation (CI job), add alert for post-rotation 5xx/401 spike, and update runbook with explicit rollback criteria.
Security will sign off on reconciliation plan prior to full cluster change.

source.summary: SCIM group displayName with emoji prevents RBAC mapping for internal console elevations
source.description:
Issue summary:
Several internal users cannot assume temporary elevated roles in the support console after being added to Okta groups whose displayName contains non-ASCII characters (emoji and mixed scripts). This surfaced after an HR group import that included emojis and slashes in group names.

Impact:
- Engineers could not escalate to runbook/incident responder roles for two oncall incidents; manual escalation caused ~10-20 minute delays.
- Affects any group where SCIM displayName includes surrogate pairs or combined unicode sequences. ASCII-only groups unaffected.

Environment:
- Okta -> SCIM sync -> internal identity-proxy (us-west).
- RBAC mapper resolves Okta group displayName to internal role grants during elevation.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create Okta group with displayName: "Incident Responders 🚨" and externalId: "incident-responders".
2) Allow SCIM incremental sync.
3) Attempt temporary elevation in support console bound to that group.
4) Observe 403 and missing mapping in rbac-mapper logs.

Observed behavior:
- rbac-mapper raises UnicodeNormalizationError while normalizing displayName.
- identity-proxy includes group claim with JSON-escaped surrogate pairs; downstream equality check fails.
- Console shows a generic "Access denied" with no normalization hint.

Expected behavior:
- RBAC mapping should use externalId as canonical mapping or reliably normalize displayName so group grants are deterministic regardless of unicode content.
investigation_notes: 2026-03-12 09:17 - Priya Nair (SRE) filed first report after failing to escalate during an incident.
2026-03-12 10:05 - Support reproduced by creating test Okta group with emoji; SCIM payload had displayName = "Incident Responders 🚨" and externalId present.
2026-03-12 10:22 - rbac-mapper stacktrace: UnicodeNormalizationError at rbac/matcher.py:142 when calling unicodedata.normalize on displayName.
2026-03-12 11:00 - identity-proxy forwarded groups claim: [{id: "incident-responders", displayName: "Incident Responders \uD83D\uDEA8"}] (note JSON-escaped sequence).
2026-03-13 08:30 - Confirmed workaround: manual alias mapping in RBAC admin restores elevations for affected group.
comments: Priya Nair (SRE) - 2026-03-12: Hit this twice during morning incident; manual escalation required.
Diego Martinez (Auth) - 2026-03-12: Investigating RBAC mapper normalization edge-case where surrogate pairs differ across services.
Ava Chen (Support) - 2026-03-13: Created temporary alias mapping for the affected responder group to restore operations.
Diego Martinez (Auth) - 2026-03-14: Built and unit-tested patch to normalize displayName with NFC and fall back to externalId when normalization fails. Preparing PR and rollout notes.
Liam O'Connor (Platform Eng) - 2026-03-14: Recommend one-off migration to create externalId-based mappings for recent non-ASCII groups.
next_steps: Merge PR #5821 to add NFC normalization and externalId-first mapping in rbac-mapper (Diego).
Run one-off migration: scan the last 14 days of SCIM imports for groups with non-ASCII displayName and create alias entries mapping externalId -> role (Platform Eng).
Update SCIM runbook and post-deploy checks to include unicode normalization validation (Docs/Identity).
Notify SRE and Support with rollback plan and maintenance window: planned 2026-03-16 02:00 UTC, 30 minutes expected.

source.summary: Suspicious mass auth attempts after internal "job application" form; possible credential harvest impacting service accounts
source.description:
Summary: On 2026-03-13 09:42 UTC the security monitoring alerting pipeline flagged a clustered spike of failed and then some successful token exchanges from several service accounts following a widely-circulated internal "job application" Google Form. The pattern suggests credentials may have been harvested via the form and subsequently used to attempt programmatic access.

Impact: Multiple service-account API tokens and a small number of CI systems observed anomalous auth activity. No confirmed data exfiltration detected so far. Some internal CI jobs failed due to token rotation triggered after suspicious logins. Customer-facing services are not known to be affected.

Detection: Real-time SIEM rules (auth-anomaly-001) and the anomaly detector for token-exchange rate detected a burst between 09:25–09:47 UTC with (>120) failed refreshes and 7 successful refreshes originating from 3 public IPs not previously seen in the org. Alert fired to #security and #oncall.

Initial hypothesis: Public Google Form (internal-only link accidentally made public) was used to collect resumes; attackers embedded a credential-harvesting redirect or 3rd-party widget that captured employee credentials. Harvested credentials then used to authenticate service principals (shared secrets stored in CI) or to exchange for short-lived tokens via our IdP.

Immediate containment actions taken:
- Rotated affected service-account keys and CI secrets for flagged projects (automated rotations executed 09:55–10:20 UTC).
- Blocked originating IPs at the cloud firewall and added them to blocklist in WAF (completed 10:05 UTC).
- Disabled the public job form and removed external sharing; notified GSuite admin to revoke any third-party app consents.
- Temporarily enforced stricter token issuance policy for our IdP for 30m window (reduced refresh window).

Next steps (short term):
- Complete audit of all service accounts that had token activity during the window (Owner: IAM team).
- Run credential exposure checks against recent form submissions (Owner: Security Ops).
- Identify whether any of the 7 successful token exchanges correspond to privilege escalation actions in Cloud Audit Logs.
- Revoke any suspicious sessions and ensure CI pipelines use rotated secrets.

Longer-term follow-ups:
- Review internal form sharing policies and update onboarding docs to require security review for externally embedded widgets.
- Add a SIEM rule to detect sudden token-exchange success rate following a high volume of failed refreshes.
- Postmortem and employee notification plan if any account compromise confirmed.

Contact: Security Ops (pager), Oncall SRE, Identity team.
steps_to_reproduce: N/A - incident involves live auth traffic; reproduction not attempted
Simulate credential harvest by submitting dummy creds to a test form with a monitored webhook (security lab only)
Trigger token-exchange metric in staging by performing repeated refresh attempts from an external IP range
logs: SIEM alert id: auth-anomaly-001 (2026-03-13 09:42 UTC)
Cloud Audit Query: project:infra AND protoPayload.methodName:TokenExchange AND timestamp>2026-03-13T09:20:00Z
Kibana: logs-auth-prod -> filter src_ip:(198.51.100.23 OR 203.0.113.45 OR 192.0.2.12) AND event.action:refresh_token
investigation_notes: 09:50 UTC - Security Ops: initial correlation shows 3 public IPs; reverse DNS lookup suggests hosting provider in country X. No lateral movement detected beyond token exchange attempts.
11:10 UTC - IAM: 12 service accounts had refresh attempts; 4 had succeeded at least once. Rotations scheduled and completed for 9:30–10:20 UTC window.
13:30 UTC - SRE: two CI jobs failing observed to be due to secret rotation; owners notified and pipelines re-run with new secrets.
15:00 UTC - Risk: no exfil events in S3 or DB audit logs matching the compromised tokens so far; continue monitoring for 72 hours.
comments: Leah Morrison (Security): Created ticket after SIEM escalated; initial containment started and rotations kicked off.
Ethan Park (Oncall SRE): Confirmed CI job failures due to secret rotation; coordinated with infra owners to re-run. Added IP block rules to firewall.
Maya Chen (IAM): Rotated keys for affected service accounts and listed impacted pipelines in the ticket. Will provide final list by EOD.
Jordan Patel (Legal): Please advise on employee notification scope if internal credential capture confirmed. Flagging for potential data incident review.
Security Ops (automated): Added watch rule to monitor token-exchange rate and will send hourly summaries to this ticket.
source.resolution: In progress - containment performed (rotations, IP blocks). Further forensic sweep and confirmation of any compromised accounts ongoing. Full postmortem planned once root cause confirmed.
source.workaround: Rotated affected secrets and blocked malicious IPs; temporarily tightened IdP refresh policy. CI owners should re-run pipelines with updated credentials.
follow_up_actions: Security to complete form-submission forensic scrape for exposed fields (due 2026-03-15)
IAM to produce master list of rotated service accounts and confirm no remaining active risky tokens (due 2026-03-14)
SRE to re-run impacted CI jobs and monitor for failures (ongoing)
Compliance to assess whether employee or customer notification is required (due 2026-03-16)

Impact: Multiple service-account API tokens and a small number of CI systems observed anomalous auth activity. No confirmed data exfiltration detected so far. Some internal CI jobs failed due to token rotation triggered after suspicious logins. Customer-facing services are not known to be affected.

Detection: Real-time SIEM rules (auth-anomaly-001) and the anomaly detector for token-exchange rate detected a burst between 09:25–09:47 UTC with (>120) failed refreshes and 7 successful refreshes originating from 3 public IPs not previously seen in the org. Alert fired to #security and #oncall.

Initial hypothesis: Public Google Form (internal-only link accidentally made public) was used to collect resumes; attackers embedded a credential-harvesting redirect or 3rd-party widget that captured employee credentials. Harvested credentials then used to authenticate service principals (shared secrets stored in CI) or to exchange for short-lived tokens via our IdP.

Immediate containment actions taken:
- Rotated affected service-account keys and CI secrets for flagged projects (automated rotations executed 09:55–10:20 UTC).
- Blocked originating IPs at the cloud firewall and added them to blocklist in WAF (completed 10:05 UTC).
- Disabled the public job form and removed external sharing; notified GSuite admin to revoke any third-party app consents.
- Temporarily enforced stricter token issuance policy for our IdP for 30m window (reduced refresh window).

Next steps (short term):
- Complete audit of all service accounts that had token activity during the window (Owner: IAM team).
- Run credential exposure checks against recent form submissions (Owner: Security Ops).
- Identify whether any of the 7 successful token exchanges correspond to privilege escalation actions in Cloud Audit Logs.
- Revoke any suspicious sessions and ensure CI pipelines use rotated secrets.

Longer-term follow-ups:
- Review internal form sharing policies and update onboarding docs to require security review for externally embedded widgets.
- Add a SIEM rule to detect sudden token-exchange success rate following a high volume of failed refreshes.
- Postmortem and employee notification plan if any account compromise confirmed.

Contact: Security Ops (pager), Oncall SRE, Identity team.

source.summary: Centralize ephemeral labeling-seat pool for LabelWave pilot (SCIM + BYO-KMS) and align renewal to FYQ3
source.description:
Issue summary:
We need to onboard vendor 'LabelWave' for a 6-week labeling pilot. The vendor requires: SCIM provisioning for ephemeral contractor accounts, BYO-KMS support for storing exported label artifacts, and a single invoice aligned to our FY Q3 renewal window.

Impact:
- Research & Data team cannot start the pilot until seat provisioning is available.
- Contractors currently require manual invites which increases onboarding time by ~2 days per contractor and creates security auditing gaps.
- If renewal dates are not aligned, we will incur multiple pro-rated invoices and extra procurement overhead.

Request / Acceptance criteria:
1) Create a centralized ephemeral license pool (50 seats) that can be handed out to contractors and reclaimed automatically via SCIM deprovisioning.
2) Configure SCIM integration with LabelWave and map attributes: email, manager, cost_center tag.
3) Validate BYO-KMS workflow (AWS KMS key ARN provided by Redwood) — ensure LabelWave can encrypt exported artifacts using our key or confirm an approved alternative.
4) Finance: route invoices to Cost Center: RDT-40 and consolidate renewal to align with Redwood FY Q3 start date (2026-07-01).
5) Security: run a short intake checklist (SaaS security questionnaire + SOC2 evidence) and provide a go/no-go.
6) Deliver a short runbook for on-call and CS to manage seat reclamation and emergency access.

Attachments (uploaded to ticket): contract_draft_LabelWave_2026-03-08.pdf; vendor_security_answers_LabelWave_2026-03-09.xlsx
source.steps_to_complete: 1) Procurement to confirm pricing and initiate PO; 2) Security to complete intake within 3 business days; 3) Infra to provision SCIM endpoint and share mappings with vendor; 4) Platform team to test BYO-KMS encrypt/decrypt flow with sample export; 5) Finance to create cost_center routing and set renewal anchor to 2026-07-01; 6) Create runbook in Confluence and share with Research & Data team.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:14 - Priya Nair (reporter): Started ticket after initial vendor call. Vendor confirmed SCIM + BYO-KMS are supported but asked for a tech contact and attribute mapping. Attached contract draft.
2026-03-10 10:02 - Ethan Park (assignee): Looping in infra and security. I will draft the SCIM attribute mapping template; please confirm the required cost_center naming convention for Finance.

2026-03-11 14:27 - Maria Gomez (Security): Received vendor questionnaire. Noted missing SOC2 certificate link — asked vendor to provide. Will run a short network/data-flow review once we have SOC2 and the encryption details.

2026-03-12 08:46 - Ben Lee (Finance): Confirm Cost Center RDT-40 is available. We can arrange consolidated billing, but procurement needs to sign off on payment terms. Recommend 30-day net; vendor prefers up-front for pilot. Will escalate if needed.
2026-03-13 16:11 - LabelWave (vendor rep, via email): Provided sample SCIM SCHEMA mapping and AWS KMS compatibility notes. They require a service account to test SCIM sync in our staging environment.

2026-03-14 09:00 - Ethan Park: Created infra PR to add LabelWave SCIM connector and initial attribute mapping. Shared PR link in related_github_prs. Assigned a short test window (2026-03-16) for SCIM sync verification.
investigation_notes: Confirmed vendor supports SCIM 2.0 and can consume a group-based ephemeral pool; they suggested 'membershipDuration' TTL but need our deprovisioning flow to enforce reclamation.

Security: vendor's encryption flow indicates support for customer-managed KMS keys via envelope encryption — Security asked for key policy template and least-privilege IAM role for LabelWave to call KMS decrypt if required.

Finance: flagged potential duplicate charges if vendor bills per-seat and also requires an up-front pilot fee. Need procurement to negotiate a single consolidated pilot invoice and credit for unused seats.

Infra: test plan drafted — create test tenant in staging, provision 5 ephemeral accounts via SCIM, run deprovision TTL to verify reclamation within 24 hours, and simulate export encrypt/decrypt using sandbox KMS key.
Risk: If BYO-KMS cannot be completed before pilot start, propose temporary escrow flow where LabelWave uses their key but we require additional contract controls and limited export access.
source.resolution: In Progress — Procurement has the signed SOW pending final payment terms. Infra PR added for SCIM mapping (see related_github_prs). Security awaits SOC2 link and KMS key policy to approve BYO-KMS. Finance confirmed cost center routing is possible. Next steps: finalize PO (Procurement), provide KMS key policy (Security), schedule SCIM integration test week of 2026-03-16.

source.summary: Restricted forensic sample extract for privacy-incident reproduction
source.description:
Issue summary:
A customer reported a potential PII leak in generated outputs from an application using redwood/longform-v1. We need a narrowly scoped forensic sample extract from production inference logs to reproduce the issue and validate whether PII appeared in model outputs.

Justification:
- Reproduction required to determine root cause (model prompt leakage vs. prompt content).
- Small, time-boxed sample minimizes exposure.

Scope of data requested:
- Time window: 2026-03-11 00:00:00 to 2026-03-11 23:59:59 (UTC)
- Up to 200 inference records matching the customer tenant and the reported conversation hashes.
- Include fields: request_id, customer_tenant_id (hashed), request_prompt (redacted), response_text (original and redacted copy), model_version, routing_info, timestamp, and system metadata (instance id, region).
- Explicit exclusion: do NOT include raw user identifiers or customer-provided file blobs.

Redaction & security controls proposed:
- Hash customer_tenant_id with HMAC-SHA256 and store salt in KMS-scoped key; original id not stored in extract.
- Run automated PII DLP scanner on response_text and mask email addresses, phone numbers, and long (>=16) numeric sequences. Keep an unmasked copy in an encrypted S3 object encrypted with customer-specific KMS key accessible only to approvers for a maximum of 7 days.
- Write-only S3 path with ACL restricted to the approved list; enable S3 object lock and server-side encryption with KMS.
- Access audit: all downloads require an IAM session with MFA and are logged to the security audit trail.

Retention & disposal:
- Extract TTL: 7 calendar days from extraction completion; automated deletion triggered by lifecycle rule.
- Any derived artifacts (screenshots, local copies) must be ephemeral and deleted after use; users must attest to deletion in this ticket.

Approval needed from:
- Security (data handling and key access)
- Legal/Privacy (customer notice & contract review)
- Engineering (SRE or runtime owner to run job)

Impact if delayed:
- Slows customer response and may escalate to incident; potential regulatory exposure if PII is confirmed.

Requested owner and run plan:
- Owner: Ethan Park (SRE) to run extraction job with help from Infra tooling.
- Run plan: run a narrow query against tensorflow-prod-logs table, filter by customer tenant hash, limit 200 rows, pipe to DLP scanner, package results to secure S3 with metadata manifest and HMAC verification.

Acceptance criteria:
- Security and Legal sign-off documented in comments.
- Extract contains only scoped fields and passes DLP masking policy.
- TTL and audit controls in place and verified.
comments: 2026-03-12 09:04 - Maya Chen: Opening ticket after receiving customer report. Provided initial scope and redaction plan. Assigned to SRE to prepare extraction playbook.

2026-03-12 11:22 - Ethan Park: Acknowledged. Will do a dry-run query on staging to validate filter and sampling. Need Security sign-off for KMS access and for placing an unmasked copy on secure bucket for reviewers.

2026-03-12 15:46 - Priya Natarajan (Security): Requested clarifications: exact list of approvers, KMS key ARN, justification for storing unmasked copy, and evidence of DLP tool config. Also asked for a minimal sampling strategy to avoid overexposure.

2026-03-13 08:13 - Maya Chen: Responding with approver list (Maya Chen, Ethan Park, Priya Natarajan, Legal: Jonah Ruiz), proposed KMS key arn: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/forensic-int-32177, and rationale for unmasked copy: required for final triage and legal review; will be deleted after 7 days. Attached staging dry-run results (link to internal artifact).

2026-03-13 10:02 - Jonah Ruiz (Legal): Running a quick contract check; if the customer agreement has a data-processing clause that allows debugging telemetry, Legal can approve with a customer notification choice. Legal requests a draft of the outbound notification and confirmation that no contractual BAA or higher restriction exists for this tenant.

2026-03-13 13:39 - Ethan Park: Performed staging dry-run and included sampling SQL in investigation_notes. Prepared extraction job but holding until Security & Legal approve KMS access and notification approach.

2026-03-14 09:50 - Priya Natarajan (Security): Provisioned temporary KMS grant to ethan. Security approves on condition that the unmasked copy is encrypted with the dedicated KMS key, access limited to approvers, and DLP scan passes prior to any downloads. Marking approval in ticket.

2026-03-14 10:05 - Jonah Ruiz (Legal): Confirmed customer contract permits telemetry-based debugging; Legal approves with condition that customer is notified with opt-out option within 48 hours after extraction completes. Added requirement to retain audit trail of notification.
2026-03-14 10:18 - Ethan Park: Starting production extraction now. Will post manifest and S3 path once upload completes.
investigation_notes: Staging dry-run query (staging table names replaced): SELECT request_id, tenant_hash, response_text, model_version, timestamp FROM inference_logs_staging WHERE tenant_hash = '<tenant_hash>' AND timestamp BETWEEN '2026-03-11T00:00:00Z' AND '2026-03-11T23:59:59Z' LIMIT 200;
Initial staging sample: 12 candidate records where response_text contains potential PII-like patterns (emails/phone). None confirmed as customer-provided content yet.

Hypotheses: (1) customer prompt included pasted contact details => not a model leakage; (2) model exposed training data artifacts under specific prompt pattern => requires broader sample analysis. Forensic extract will help distinguish.
DLP config: run 'dlp-scan-v2' that masks emails using regex and flags high-entropy numeric sequences. Verify mask_count == 0 before allowing approvers to download unmasked artifact.
Extraction script location: github.com/redwood/infrastructure/forensics/scripts/extract_prod_samples.sh (PR pending: https://github.com/redwood/infrastructure/pull/842)
S3 upload target (write-only): s3://redwood-secure/forensic/INT-32177/ (server-side-encryption: aws:kms, kms-key: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/forensic-int-32177)
Approvers list: Maya Chen (reporter), Ethan Park (SRE), Priya Natarajan (Security), Jonah Ruiz (Legal).
source.resolution_notes: 2026-03-14: Security and Legal approved the extraction with conditions. SRE ran the extraction job and uploaded two objects: masked_results.json (masked) and encrypted_unmasked_results.enc (KMS-wrapped) to s3://redwood-secure/forensic/INT-32177/. Extraction produced 87 records; DLP scan flagged 5 records which were masked in masked_results.json. Access granted to approvers only via temporary IAM session. TTL configured to delete objects after 7 days (lifecycle rule). Customer notification template drafted by Legal and queued for sending; audit trail entry created in the security log. Follow-up action: add a pre-filter runbook to annotate suspicious prompts at ingestion and schedule a post-incident review to consider automated prompt redaction (owner: Product Security).

source.summary: Provisioning/Repair: Fleet power policy rollout locks CPU at low P-state causing Windows container start failures
source.description:
Issue summary:
A recent Intune-driven corporate power policy rollout applied to a subset of developer and infra workstations appears to be forcing minimum processor states and other AC power parameters that lock CPU performance at the lowest P-state on some systems. Affected machines show sustained ~400 MHz CPU frequency on AC power after provisioning/re-imaging which causes Windows containers (Docker Desktop and Windows Server containers used for local dev) to fail health checks and refuse to start reliably.

Impact:
- Several internal engineers and platform developers cannot start local containers; CI jobs that rely on Windows container hosts fail on self-hosted runners on affected machines.
- Issue observed on ~12 confirmed devices in the us-west fleet; likely more unreported.
- Productivity impact (blocked local dev, intermittent CI failures). No customer-facing outage.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 22H2 (corporate image)
source.steps_to_reproduce:
1) Fresh image using corp provisioning image (SCCM/MDT) with Intune enrollment.
2) Device receives Intune Configuration Profile 'Corporate Power - v3' applied during autopilot/check-in.
3) Reboot as prompted.
4) Launch Docker Desktop (or attempt to run Windows container). Observe CPU freq and container failure.
source.observed_behavior:
- CPU frequency reported locked at ~350-600 MHz in Task Manager and Intel Power Gadget on AC power.
- Docker containers fail during init with timeout on health probes; Docker Desktop reports repeated OOM/exit errno related to slow startup.
- Windows event log: Kernel-Power entries referencing 'Processor performance decreased due to power policy change' and entries from the 'Power' provider indicating min processor state set to 1-3%.
source.expected_behavior:
- Machines should use normal P-state scaling on AC power (turbo/boost available).
- Containers should start and pass healthchecks within normal time window.
source.investigation_notes:
- Reproduced on a repro lab machine by assigning the same Intune profile: after profile application the 'Minimum processor state' AC value was set to 1% and the 'System cooling policy' was set to passive.
- The change appears to be delivered by an Intune Power CSP (Configuration Profile) that sets registry keys under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power.
- On affected machines Dell Power Manager (and on some Lenovo machines 'Lenovo Power Manager') shows a conflict: vendor power utilities are also applying firmware-level power caps during platform initialization.
- Hypothesis: combination of aggressive min-processor-state + vendor firmware caps results in CPU stuck in low P-state and Windows PPM not recovering after resume/reboot.
- Sysinternals pslist and perfmon show CPU core frequencies never rising under load; stress tests do not increase frequency.
- We compared to an unaffected machine where the Intune profile had not yet applied: same image, different timing — processor scales normally.
source.temporary_workaround:
Manual remediation steps that have restored functionality:
1) Run (elevated) 'powercfg -restoredefaultschemes' then 'powercfg /setacvalueindex scheme_current SUB_PROCESSOR PROCTHROTTLEMIN 5' and set to 5-10% for affected machines.
2) Uninstall vendor power manager (Dell/Lenovo) where practical then reboot.
3) Reverting the Intune profile to previous version (v2) on a test device prevented the regression.
Notes: These are temporary and not feasible at scale as-is.
source.root_cause_analysis: Preliminary: A new corporate Intune power policy (Corporate Power - v3) inadvertently set minimum processor state and passive cooling aggressively to maximize battery/thermal longevity for remote/hybrid users. On several OEM platforms a concurrent vendor firmware/agent enforces a low-power cap at boot. When both changes are present, Windows PPM fails to escalate P-states under load. The combined effect prevents CPU turbo and makes time-sensitive container startup fail.
source.next_steps:
- Roll back Corporate Power - v3 in Intune for 'developer' device group and re-evaluate. (Owner: Endpoint team)
- Update the Intune profile to remove aggressive min-processor-state and avoid touching System Cooling Policy; publish as Corporate Power - v3.1. (Owner: Endpoint + Security)
- Create targeted remediation plan: Intune Win32 remediation script to detect affected devices and run powercfg fixes automatically. (Owner: SRE/Endpoint)
- Engage OEM firmware team to confirm vendor agent interaction and whether BIOS microcode aggression is expected. (Owner: Firmware)
- Run fleet-wide scan and add label to asset database for devices that applied v3 during rollout window (2026-03-11 to 2026-03-12).
source.resolution_notes: (Pending) Rolling back policy for developer pool mitigates the issue; automated remediation script is being validated in staging.
comments:
2026-03-12 09:14 - Priya Shah: Reported after two engineers in infra-team couldn't run local Windows containers following overnight provisioning. Attaching initial repro steps and Task Manager screenshots in ticket.
2026-03-12 11:03 - Ethan Park: Assigned. Reproduced on Dell 7440 in lab. Confirmed power profile changes via registry. Escalating to Endpoint team to hold rollout.
2026-03-12 14:22 - Maya Rodriguez (Endpoint): We've temporarily paused Intune profile v3 assignment to 'Engineering - dev' group while we verify impact. Drafting v3.1 with conservative P-state values.
2026-03-13 08:47 - Tomas Nguyen (Firmware): Observed that vendor power agent on HP/Lenovo may set ACPI _PDC hints that bias platform to low-power; collecting firmware versions and contacting vendor.
2026-03-14 10:05 - Ethan Park: Created remediation script (win32app) that runs powercfg restore and sets min proc state to 10% and removes passive cooling override. Validating on 5 repro devices.

- Productivity impact (blocked local dev, intermittent CI failures). No customer-facing outage.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 22H2 (corporate image)

source.summary: Self-hosted runners unable to upload job artifacts after storage service-account key rotation
source.summary_details: After a scheduled rotation of the GCS service-account JSON key in Vault, a subset of self-hosted release runners started failing artifact uploads with 403/permission errors. New jobs can start, but steps that persist artifacts to GCS fail consistently on affected runners.
source.impact: Several release pipelines (mainly canary and nightly builds) are failing at the artifact upload step. End-to-end CI completes for ~60% of jobs; ~40% fail depending on runner pool. This is impacting release cadence for two teams and blocking one canary rollout.
source.steps_to_reproduce: 1) Schedule a job on an affected self-hosted runner pool (label: release-runner). 2) Run a job that produces artifacts and pushes to GCS via the platform artifact uploader. 3) Observe upload step fail with 403 and 'permission denied' in upload logs.
source.observed_logs:
upload_error: google.api_core.exceptions.Forbidden: 403 The caller does not have permission
  at artifact_client.upload (uploader.go:142)
  Runner agent logs: token refresh ok; vault fetch success; gcloud auth activate-service-account failed with: 'invalid_grant' in a small subset of requests.
source.initial_hypothesis: Rotation of the GCS service-account key introduced a timing window where some runner agents kept a cached, now-revoked credential and attempted to use it to obtain short-lived tokens; cluster-level RBAC or IAM binding change might have been applied concurrently, causing inconsistent permission sets across runner pods.
source.investigation_notes: - Vault rotation job executed 2026-03-12T03:10Z and wrote new key:ss: gcs-release-sa. - We observed two behaviors: (A) runners that restarted after rotation pick up new key and succeed; (B) runners that continued running show vault fetch success but gcloud auth operations intermittently fail with 'invalid_grant' or 403 when exchanging keys. - No global IAM role changes detected in cloud audit logs at rotation time. - Artifact uploader config unchanged. - Affected runner pods share the same node pools as healthy ones; not a node-level network issue. - Related Slack thread: #ci-ops (link). - Linked issue INT-48917 (ephemeral-runner-vault-rewrap-decrypt-failure) investigated vault rewrap before; not identical but useful for pattern comparison.
source.reproduction_attempts: We reproduced the failure by running a small job on a pre-rotation runner (did not restart runner), confirming that artifact upload fails until runner process is restarted. Restarting the runner agent pod resolves the error in reproduced runs.
source.short_term_workaround: - Restart runner agent process on affected hosts (kubectl rollout restart daemonset/release-runner-agent) to force a fresh VAULT fetch and re-auth. - For critical release pipelines, temporarily route jobs to runner pools that have been rotated and restarted. - Notify teams of transient failures and recommended rerun after restart.
source.proposed_fix: 1) Patch the runner agent to detect service-account key rotation events in Vault and automatically re-auth without a full pod restart (hot-reload). 2) Add a post-rotation orchestrator step that performs a rolling restart of runner agents after a key rotation completes. 3) Add end-to-end test in rotation pipeline to validate artifact uploads after key rotate.
source.rollout_plan_and_risks: - Implement hot-reload in agent (small binary change) and ship to staging. - Run key rotation in staging and validate. - Deploy to prod behind a staged rollout (10% -> 50% -> 100%) with monitoring. Risks: if hot-reload has an edge-case, some agents may still require restart; fallback is rolling restart orchestrator.
source.mitigation_done: - Performed targeted rolling restart of release-runner-agent on 8 affected hosts, which restored artifact uploads for ~95% of failing jobs. - Updated rotation runbook to include mandatory runner agent restart step until hot-reload is implemented.
source.resolution_eta: Hot-reload agent patch target: 2026-03-20. Complete rollout and rotation automation update by 2026-03-24.
source.follow_up_actions: - (OWNER: DevInfra) Implement agent hot-reload and add unit/integration tests (PR TBD). - (OWNER: CI Ops) Update rotation runbook to include canary restarts and validation job. - (OWNER: Security) Review rotation procedure to ensure zero-window guarantees if possible. - Create postmortem if production impact > 4 hours.
source.notes: Customer-impacting? Internal only. Affected teams: Release Eng, Platform, Security. Logged to #ci-ops and #incidents. Will escalate to SEV1 if more than two release pipelines are blocked beyond 6 hours.

source.summary: TTPO deprovision: staggered SSO retirement, MDM re-enroll, and courier-hold for cross-border departure
source.description:
Issue summary:
HR has requested a two-week temporary passover (TTPO) deprovision for an employee relocating overseas mid-contract. Request is to stagger account deactivation so service accounts and SSO remain available for knowledge-transfer tasks until the courier confirms laptop collection. HR flagged export/compliance concerns for the laptop transit and asked Asset Management to place a hold with the courier.

Impact:
- Potential risk of accidental early SSO/account revocation interrupting handover work (engineering + documentation updates).
- Hardware custody must remain in chain-of-custody due to encryption keys tied to device KMS tags.
- Cross-border courier requires explicit KYC and export paperwork before release.

Environment:
- Internal accounts: SSO (Okta), GSuite, GitHub org, AWS console role.
- Device managed by MDM (Jamf).
- Employee tier: full-time engineering (access to staging and prod service accounts).

Requested outcome:
1) Create a hard stop plan that defers destructive deprovisioning actions for two weeks (TTPO window) and documents which identities are delayed and which are removed immediately.
2) Coordinate with Asset Management to create a courier hold (do not release) with a named carrier and tracking, including export paperwork checklist.
3) Re-enroll the device into a custody MDM profile if courier requires device reset prior to shipping.
4) Schedule final deprovision (full SSO removal, KMS key rotation, service-account handback) upon confirmation of device receipt and HR sign-off.

Background and constraints:
- Employee will be physically leaving the country on 2026-03-20; HR requested a TTPO window through 2026-03-27 to finish handover tasks.
- Security policy requires device rekey when device leaves company control for >72 hours. We need to reconcile that with the TTPO exception.
- Legal requires export checklist for this country; Asset Management has initiated a case with logistics partner but needs support from Security and Compliance for documentation.

Attachments:
- HR exit form (attached in HR drive link)
- Logistics pre-check PDF (uploaded to ticket)

Notes for assignees: please avoid immediate deactivation of roles that would break CI or production runbooks; instead rotate service account keys where possible and freeze destructive IAM role deprovision until final closure.
steps_to_complete: HR to confirm TTPO end date and sign-off owner (HR contact: L. Patel)
IAM: create staged deprovision plan listing identities to retain (e.g., Okta session active, GitHub org membership), and identities to retire immediately (personal tokens)
MDM: apply custody profile and verify encryption status; if required, schedule supervised reset only after legal approval
Asset Mgmt: place courier hold and provide carrier name, tracking placeholder, and KYC requirements
Security: prepare KMS rekey runbook and identify required service-account rotations post-receipt
On final receipt: execute KMS rekey, rotate CI service-account keys, revoke SSO sessions and close ticket
comments: Ava Chen - 2026-03-12: Opening ticket after HR request. Priority set to P1 due to cross-border/export considerations. Assigning to Diego for IAM/MDM coordination.
Diego Morales - 2026-03-12: Acknowledged. Will draft staged deprovision plan. Asking Security for KMS rekey checklist and Asset team for courier hold contact details.

Maya Singh (Security) - 2026-03-13: We can delay destructive KMS rotation until device is confirmed received. Interim mitigation: rotate service-account keys and revoke long-lived tokens. I will attach the KMS runbook link.

Jordan Li (Asset Mgmt) - 2026-03-13: Placed a temporary hold with carrier 'TransGlobal Express' — provisional tracking TGX-0000-XXXXX. Export paperwork: pending Compliance sign-off. Do not ship until Compliance confirms.
L. Patel (HR) - 2026-03-13: Confirmed TTPO window 2026-03-20 to 2026-03-27. HR will not instruct early revocation unless there is a security incident.
Diego Morales - 2026-03-14: Drafted staged deprovision plan uploaded to ticket. Next: Security to approve KMS exception; Asset to confirm carrier paperwork. Assigned subtasks to Maya and Jordan.
investigation_notes: Checked current Okta session timeout policies: sessions persist 14 days by default; will not auto-expire during TTPO window.
User has two provisioning keys in CI pipelines; both are long-lived tokens — recommended to rotate now and replace with short-lived ephemeral tokens for handover tasks.
MDM shows device encryption enabled and last check-in 2026-03-10; Jamf indicates supervised status false (needs custody profile to be applied).
Compliance: export restrictions may require manual customs declaration and additional hold; estimated processing time 3-5 business days.
No active production SRE runbooks reference the user's personal account, but the user is part of a small service ownership group — recommend temporary access handover to a named backup.
source.resolution: Workaround in place: Asset Management placed a courier hold and provided provisional tracking; IAM rotated long-lived tokens used by the employee's CI jobs and added a short-lived access token for the TTPO window. Security approved deferring KMS rekey until device receipt. Final resolution pending courier confirmation and Compliance paperwork completion.
next_steps: Compliance to confirm export paperwork (owner: Compliance Team) — due 2026-03-16
Asset Mgmt to confirm carrier tracking and pickup appointment (owner: Jordan Li) — due 2026-03-18
Security to prepare KMS rekey runbook and stand by for execution upon device receipt (owner: Maya Singh) — hold until pickup confirmation
IAM to finalize staged deprovision manifest and notify backup owners to accept responsibilities (owner: Diego Morales) — due 2026-03-16
HR to confirm final sign-off once device is received (owner: L. Patel) — TTPO end 2026-03-27

source.summary: Okta SAML session affinity breaks cross-account console switching
source.description: Issue summary: Several engineers and on-call have reported that when they use the console's 'Switch account' feature after authenticating via Okta SAML, they are immediately redirected back to the Okta login flow or see a 401 in the console even though Okta indicates an active session. This appears to be a session affinity / cookie routing problem that surfaces only when switching between org accounts (cross-account switch) or when requests are routed to different backends behind the auth-proxy.
source.impact: Multiple internal teams (SRE, Platform, and Support) and on-call users are unable to switch accounts reliably in the console. Breakage affects productivity for engineers who operate across multiple customer accounts and prevents some on-call flows that require quick org switching. Estimated ~15-25% of cross-account switch attempts fail during the regression window.
source.steps_to_reproduce:
1) Login to console via Okta SAML in Chrome (prod-us-west)
2) From the top-right, choose 'Switch account' -> select a different org (owned by the same user)
3) Observe console goes to Okta and then returns either to a blank console screen or shows '401 Unauthorized' in the network tab
4) Check cookies: auth-session cookie value differs before and after switch; RelayState is present but new session is created on different backend
5) Repeat in incognito or after clearing cookies -> behavior changes (sometimes works).
source.logs:
- auth-proxy logs (2026-03-10 09:12:43 UTC): 'saml: assertion accepted; session_id=abc123; backend=grpc-7'
- shortly after (09:12:44 UTC): 'request: /switch-account -> routed to grpc-3; cookie lookup failed; creating ephemeral session def=tmp-9'
- browser console: XHR /api/v1/auth/session -> 401 (invalid_session)
- haproxy logs show differing server selection for subsequent requests from same client IP during the switch operation
source.hypothesis: During a deploy (2026-03-09 23:00 UTC) the load-balancer sticky session settings were temporarily relaxed to allow rolling restarts. The console's account-switch flow relies on a transient in-memory session reference on the backend (short-lived state keyed to RelayState). If subsequent requests land on a different backend that doesn’t have that ephemeral state, SAML response handling fails and the user is sent back to Okta or receives a 401. Additionally, a SameSite cookie attribute change in a recent auth-proxy PR may make the browser drop the session cookie on the redirect to RelayState under certain conditions.
source.investigation_notes:
- Reproduced with engineer Chrome profile (prod-us-west) using devtools; confirmed that RelayState token was posted to different backend node than initial SAML initiate.
- Checked deploy history: auth-proxy rollout 2026-03-09 22:50–23:05 included lb config tweak for draining behavior (PR referenced above).
- Load balancer (HAProxy) metrics show short-term increase in backend selection churn during rollout window.
- Session-store is primarily in-memory with memcache fallback; ephemeral RelayState objects are not persisted to memcached (design choice to reduce latency).
- A/B test altering SameSite=None for auth-session cookie landed in auth-proxy PR #842 which affected Chrome behavior for cross-site POSTs used by RelayState.
- Reproduced failure pattern when auth-session cookie had SameSite=Lax. With SameSite=None the switch succeeds more reliably, but non-sticky routing still breaks when ephemeral RelayState is missing.
source.workarounds:
- Short-term: instruct affected engineers to open a new browser session (incognito) and perform the switch; this forces a fresh session that completes the flow.
- Admin workaround: disable cross-account quick-switch and perform full logout/login to move accounts.
- On-call note: If a 401 occurs during a critical incident, open console in incognito and perform full SSO to the desired org.
source.proposed_fix: 1) Persist RelayState tokens to memcached for 60s so any backend can handle the SAML response. 2) Revert the temporary LB sticky-session relaxation and ensure HAProxy maintain server affinity during auth flows. 3) Adjust auth-session cookie attributes to SameSite=None; secure; samesite flag behavior to be validated across browsers. 4) Add end-to-end integration test that simulates cross-account switch under non-sticky routing and validates RelayState resolution.
source.actions_taken:
- Rolled back the LB drain setting to previous stable behavior at 2026-03-12 02:10 UTC (config rollback).
- Opened PR to persist RelayState to memcached (auth-proxy PR #842) — awaiting code review.
- Temporarily enabled a proxy-level sticky header (x-auth-stick) to force affinity for SAML POST flows (deployed to prod-us-west canary at 2026-03-13 18:30 UTC).
- Notified on-call and SRE via #support and #platform Slack channels with mitigation steps and link to this ticket.
comments: 2026-03-10 10:02 - Support (Ava Chen): Initial report from Jacob M. — multiple engineers hitting 401 when switching orgs. Attached console HAR and screenshots.
2026-03-10 11:15 - SRE (Marcus Patel): I can reproduce; adding traces and checking haproxy. Suspect affinity. Pinning as P1.
2026-03-11 08:40 - Auth Eng (Sofia Martinez): Found auth-proxy change that touched SameSite. Creating a PR to add memcache fallback for RelayState. See PR #842.
2026-03-12 03:05 - SRE (Marcus Patel): Rolled back lb change that coincided with spike. Partial improvement, but still seeing intermittent failures in us-east. Starting canary sticky header.
2026-03-13 19:05 - Support (Ava Chen): Users on-call report much better behavior after canary; continuing monitoring.
2026-03-14 09:20 - QA (Ethan Rao): Added automated test scenario to console E2E that simulates cross-account switch across multiple backends (test id: e2e-switch-104).
source.resolution: In progress — temporary mitigations (LB rollback and x-auth-stick header) reduced failures to <1% in us-west. The PR to persist RelayState to memcached (auth-proxy) is in review; once merged we'll schedule a controlled rollout and monitor. The definitive fix will be deployment of memcached-backed RelayState plus SameSite cookie adjustments. After rollout we will run the new e2e test and keep the sticky header as fallback for 72 hours.
source.resolution_notes:
- memcached persistence design approved by Security (no PII stored; tokens expire after 60s).
- Auth Eng to merge PR after two approvals; target deploy window 2026-03-17 01:00 UTC with canary.
- Post-deploy: run synthetic cross-account switch test and monitor 15m error rate; if stable, remove x-auth-stick header 48h after deploy.
- Communicate to internal users via #platform that cross-account switching should be stable after 2026-03-17 UTC rollout.
follow_up_tasks: Merge auth-proxy RelayState memcache PR and deploy to prod canary
Update HAProxy runbook to avoid relaxing affinity during auth deploys
Add synthetic E2E test to CI to catch regressions

source.summary: Temporary telemetry query privilege for data-minimization audit
source.description:
Issue summary:
Request from Compliance (Privacy) to run a constrained set of telemetry queries against production request logs to confirm that new data-minimization rules have reduced retention of user identifiers in prefix caches. This is not an export request; Compliance needs aggregated metrics and a small number of sampled rows to validate masking rules.

Background:
- New data-minimization policy shipped 2026-03-05 adjusted KV cache retention and redaction on session keys.
- Compliance requires a short verification window to confirm no PII leakage in the last 7 days of production telemetry.

Impact / Risk:
- High sensitivity: production logs contain request metadata and potentially user-provided content.
- Goal is to avoid broad access to raw payloads and ensure least-privilege.

Requested access:
- JIT (just-in-time) query privilege for Compliance engineer: Duration 4 hours, single session, scoped to aggregated queries + up to 10 sampled rows with deterministic redaction applied.
- Queries will run through the observability query-console behind bastion with session recording and enforced masking UDFs.

Justification and minimality plan:
- Compliance will only need: counts, distinct user_id counts, percentage of requests containing raw email patterns, and up to 10 sampled requests where identifiers are replaced with stable tokens (tokenization) and free-text redaction applied.
- No full payload dumps, no key-value cache export. If any sample row contains unexpected PII after redaction, session is terminated and incident escalated.

Access controls and safeguards requested:
- Role granted: telemetry_read_jit (timebound IAM role that maps to a short-lived certificate).
- Network: access only via secure bastion (AUDIT-LOGS flagged), from Compliance laptop (registered device).
- Query pre-approval: Compliance to provide queries in advance. Only pre-approved queries allowed; ad-hoc queries blocked by policy.
- Data transformations: apply built-in redaction UDFs to sampled rows and mask user identifiers in the result set.
- Audit: all queries and results captured in the audit trail; copies retained for 90 days in encrypted bucket.

Duration and rollback:
- Access window: 2026-03-16 09:00 to 2026-03-16 13:00 US/Eastern (4 hours).
- If any policy violation or unexpected PII is observed, access revoked immediately and IR lead notified.

Acceptance criteria / Success criteria:
- Compliance obtains aggregated metrics showing reduction in identifier retention according to the new policy across the last 7 days.
- Sampled rows (max 10) show tokenized identifiers and redacted free text.
- No raw payloads or unredacted identifiers are exported.

Attachments (queries to run):
1) COUNT(*) by day for last 7 days with prefix-cache-hits filter.
2) DISTINCT(user_id) count and histogram of session length.
3) Regex-based scan for email patterns; return counts and top-5 matching request IDs (masked).
4) Return up to 10 sampled rows after redaction UDF applied (sampling seeded for reproducibility).

Next steps requested from SRE/Security:
- Create timebound IAM role and JIT certificate for the compliance principal.
- Pre-register the bastion session with recording enabled and notify IR lead.
- Validate redaction UDFs with a dry-run on staging using synthetic data prior to production run.
source.investigation_notes:
2026-03-12: Initial triage by Privacy -> identified required telemetry tables and redaction UDFs.
2026-03-13: SRE validated that query-console supports pre-approved query whitelist; verified session recording hooks.
2026-03-14: Security ran a dry-run of redaction UDF on synthetic dataset; one regex tweak applied to email mask.
comments: Priya Nair (Reporter) - 2026-03-12: Filing formal JIT access request on behalf of Privacy. Attached intended queries and justification.
Ethan Park (Assignee, SRE) - 2026-03-13: Confirmed observability console can enforce a whitelist and timebox roles. Preparing IAM flow; need device fingerprint from Compliance engineer.

Maya Singh (Security) - 2026-03-14: Performed dry-run of UDF redaction on staging. Email regex adjusted to avoid false positives on code snippets. Recommend 4-hour window and session recording. Will produce short runbook for the session.
Jordan Lee (Privacy) - 2026-03-14: Confirmed queries; Compliance laptop fingerprint submitted via security portal. Will standby for scheduled window.
Ethan Park - 2026-03-14: IAM role telemetry_read_jit created and scheduled. Bastion session pre-registered. Awaiting final signoff to enable access on 2026-03-16 09:00 ET.
follow_up_actions: Create post-run verification checklist and upload results to Confluence with redaction summary
Add automated regression check to telemetry pipelines to alert if retention regressions exceed thresholds

Impact / Risk:
- High sensitivity: production logs contain request metadata and potentially user-provided content.
- Goal is to avoid broad access to raw payloads and ensure least-privilege.

Requested access:
- JIT (just-in-time) query privilege for Compliance engineer: Duration 4 hours, single session, scoped to aggregated queries + up to 10 sampled rows with deterministic redaction applied.
- Queries will run through the observability query-console behind bastion with session recording and enforced masking UDFs.

Justification and minimality plan:
- Compliance will only need: counts, distinct user_id counts, percentage of requests containing raw email patterns, and up to 10 sampled requests where identifiers are replaced with stable tokens (tokenization) and free-text redaction applied.
- No full payload dumps, no key-value cache export. If any sample row contains unexpected PII after redaction, session is terminated and incident escalated.

Access controls and safeguards requested:
- Role granted: telemetry_read_jit (timebound IAM role that maps to a short-lived certificate).
- Network: access only via secure bastion (AUDIT-LOGS flagged), from Compliance laptop (registered device).
- Query pre-approval: Compliance to provide queries in advance. Only pre-approved queries allowed; ad-hoc queries blocked by policy.
- Data transformations: apply built-in redaction UDFs to sampled rows and mask user identifiers in the result set.
- Audit: all queries and results captured in the audit trail; copies retained for 90 days in encrypted bucket.

Duration and rollback:
- Access window: 2026-03-16 09:00 to 2026-03-16 13:00 US/Eastern (4 hours).
- If any policy violation or unexpected PII is observed, access revoked immediately and IR lead notified.

Acceptance criteria / Success criteria:
- Compliance obtains aggregated metrics showing reduction in identifier retention according to the new policy across the last 7 days.
- Sampled rows (max 10) show tokenized identifiers and redacted free text.
- No raw payloads or unredacted identifiers are exported.

Attachments (queries to run):
1) COUNT(*) by day for last 7 days with prefix-cache-hits filter.
2) DISTINCT(user_id) count and histogram of session length.
3) Regex-based scan for email patterns; return counts and top-5 matching request IDs (masked).
4) Return up to 10 sampled rows after redaction UDF applied (sampling seeded for reproducibility).

Next steps requested from SRE/Security:
- Create timebound IAM role and JIT certificate for the compliance principal.
- Pre-register the bastion session with recording enabled and notify IR lead.
- Validate redaction UDFs with a dry-run on staging using synthetic data prior to production run.

source.summary: GCP Workload Identity federation -> AWS assume-role: session-tag truncation causing KMS key policy denies during Dedicated provisioning
source.description:
Issue summary:
During automated Dedicated capacity provisioning for customer-tenant 'acme-ml', the cross-cloud credential broker (GCP Workload Identity Federation -> AWS STS) succeeded but downstream KMS operations failed with AccessDenied. Impact: Dedicated provisioner could not finish creating reserved clusters for two customers, blocking onboarding and test traffic.

Impact:
- 2 Dedicated provisioning runs failed in us-east region (customer-facing).
- Provisioner left partial resources (EC2 instances created without required KMS-wrapped secrets).
- Oncall had to rollback an attempted retry causing extra cost.

Environment:
- Provisioner service: prod-us-east namespace
- Broker: gcp-wif-broker (uses service account svc-provisioner@redwood-prod.iam.gserviceaccount.com)
- AWS role: arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/redwood-dedicated-provisioner
- KMS key: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:222222222222:key/aa11-bb22-cc33

Expected behavior:
- Broker issues STS token with session tags (team, tenant, request_id); AWS assume-role propagates tags and KMS key policy allows Encrypt/Decrypt when aws:PrincipalTag/team == "platform" or tenant == "acme-ml".

Observed behavior:
- STS assume-role succeeded (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) but subsequent KMS Encrypt calls return AccessDenied: "User: arn:aws:sts::222222222222:assumed-role/redwood-dedicated-provisioner/session is not authorized to perform: kms:Encrypt on resource ... because condition aws:PrincipalTag/tenant missing"
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use GCP service account svc-provisioner@redwood-prod to request OIDC token for workload identity federation.
2) Call AWS STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity including >6 session tags (tenant, team, request_id, env, feature_flag, region, extra_tag) via provisioner codepath.
3) With returned creds, call KMS:Encrypt against customer key that has condition checking aws:PrincipalTag/tenant.
4) Observe AccessDenied in CloudTrail and provisioner failure.
logs: CloudTrail: AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity: Success - RequestedSessionTags: {tenant=acme-ml, team=platform, request_id=pr-9857, env=prod, feature_flag=dedicated, region=us-east-1, extra_tag=xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx}

CloudTrail: KMS Encrypt: AccessDenied - Error: User arn:aws:sts::222222222222:assumed-role/redwood-dedicated-provisioner/session is not authorized to perform: kms:Encrypt on resource arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:222222222222:key/aa11-bb22-cc33 due to failed condition: aws:PrincipalTag/tenant
GCP Broker logs (gcp-wif-broker): TokenSize=3792 bytes, TruncatedTagsCount=2, Warning: session tag payload exceeds AWS STS max size; dropped last 2 tags
Provisioner: Error: provisioning aborted after KMS failure; partial infra left (i-0abcd1234).
investigation_notes: 03-10 09:12 - Reproduced in staging by creating OIDC token with many session tags; observed identical KMS denial when tags were dropped.

03-10 11:00 - AWS STS enforces a max size for session tags and will silently drop tags if token payload exceeds limit; keys after a certain index were missing in assumed-role session (confirmed in CloudTrail).
03-11 14:30 - KMS key policy uses aws:PrincipalTag/tenant condition to scope access to tenant-specific keys. If 'tenant' tag missing, KMS denies even if caller is assumed-role principal.

03-12 08:05 - Provisioner recently added extra session tags (feature_flag and extra_tag) in PR 439 to carry trace metadata. This increased session tag payload size and pushed 'tenant' tag past the truncation boundary in some calls.
03-13 16:20 - Confirmed with AWS Support that when tag payload exceeds STS limit, AWS may drop a subset of tags without failing the AssumeRole; there is no server-side error in that case.
Hypothesis: Addition of non-essential tags caused session payload > limit for some requests, causing tenant tag to be dropped leading to KMS denial.
comments: Ava Chen (reporter) 2026-03-10: First observed during dedicated provisioning for acme-ml; escalation requested.
Miguel Alvarez (assignee) 2026-03-10: Acknowledged - assigning to SRE + Auth to investigate token size and tag propagation.
SRE-Lead (comment) 2026-03-11: Confirmed CloudTrail shows missing PrincipalTag keys on assumed role. Captured full STS request/response for sample trace id pr-9857.
Auth-Engineer (comment) 2026-03-12: We reproduced in staging. Suspect session tag ordering in caller code matters; recommend moving tenant/tag keys up earlier and dropping non-essential tags.
Miguel Alvarez 2026-03-13: Coordinated with Provisioner owners to open PR to remove extra_tag and always emit tenant as first tag. Built hotfix branch for immediate rollout.
Provisioner-Owner 2026-03-14: Hotfix merged and deployed to prod canary; provisioning for acme-ml retried and completed successfully. Monitoring for two hours.
resolution_notes: Root cause: Provisioner started sending additional non-essential session tags which increased the OIDC/session payload beyond AWS STS tagging size threshold. AWS truncated the tail of session tags; the tenant tag was sometimes dropped, causing KMS key policy conditions to fail and produce AccessDenied on kms:Encrypt.

Immediate mitigation: Reverted the non-essential tag (extra_tag) and reordered tags in the provisioner code so tenant and team are emitted first. Deployed hotfix to prod canary on 2026-03-14 08:40 UTC and rolled to full prod after 2-hour verification window.

Long-term remediation actions:
- PROV-214: Add validation in provisioner CI to ensure total session tag payload stays under AWS documented limits and fail PRs that exceed the threshold.
- AUTH-59: Add instrumentation to gcp-wif-broker to report dropped tags count as a metric and alert on >0 drops in prod.
- SEC-87: Update KMS key policies to include a fallback allow for the provisioner assumed-role (with tight conditions) to reduce single-point failure mode while other mitigations land.
- Runbook update: Confluence runbook updated with troubleshooting steps for session tag truncation and checklist to check CloudTrail for missing aws:PrincipalTag entries.
Status: Resolved for the immediate customer onboarding runs. Follow-up work assigned to Provisioner and Auth teams with tickets PROV-214, AUTH-59, SEC-87 linked.

source.summary: Windows 11 roaming + adapter power-save causing WireGuard route ghosting and DNS failures on corporate Wi‑Fi
source.description:
Issue summary:
Windows 11 laptops intermittently lose connectivity to internal services after roaming between APs. The WireGuard interface remains up but the system installs a stale 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing at the local wifi gateway (metric low), effectively blackholing outbound traffic for processes bound to the wg interface. DNS queries either fail or go to the wrong resolver.

Impact:
- ~8 active users in the SF office reporting repeated failures during heavy roaming (moving between floors / boardroom).
- Symptoms: applications time out (internal dashboards, git pushes), developer shells lose connections, Slack remains connected but messages to internal services fail.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 22H2 (build 22631.x)
- Wireless chipset: Intel AX211 / AX201 (driver 22.50.1 reported on affected machines)
- VPN: WireGuard v0.7.6 (enterprise config via enterprise-agent)
- Zero-trust/agent: redwood-edge v2.1.0 (manages wg interface and DNS overrides)
- SSID: Redwood-Corp (802.11ax, controller-managed fast-roaming/FT enabled)

Steps to reproduce:
1) Connect to Redwood-Corp from a Windows 11 laptop with WireGuard active.
2) Walk between two nearby APs or trigger a roam using the vendor utility (force roam).
3) Observe that user retains WPA2/WPA3 session but some processes lose access to internal services.
4) Run 'wg show', 'route print', and 'nslookup internal-service'.

Observed behavior / logs (excerpt):
- route print shows 0.0.0.0 -> 192.168.10.1 metric 10 (preferred) coexisting with wg0 route 0.0.0.0 -> 10.10.0.1 metric 50; processes bound to wg0 still attempt outbound but packets never egress correctly.
- WireGuard peers show keepalives delayed; 'wg show' shows handshake timestamps stall for 30–120s.
- Windows System event: WLAN AutoConfig reported 'Roaming completed' then 'Driver queued packets during power save transition'.
- tcpdump on edge gateway shows missing keepalives during DTIM handover windows.

Initial hypothesis:
- Wireless driver power-management / DTIM handling during fast roaming causes packet queuing and delayed keepalives; WireGuard's endpoint detection thinks tunnel still healthy but routing table gets re-ordered when interface metrics are recalculated.
- Controller fast-roaming timers (FT) combined with Windows power profile and driver implementation create a race where the default route is re-inserted before wg interface routes stabilize.

Impact assessment:
- P1 due to multiple developers blocked during peak hours; intermittent but reproducible in SF office.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced on 2 lab machines with same Intel AX211 driver.
- Disabling 'Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power' on the WLAN adapter immediately eliminated the symptom on test devices.
- Changing Windows Power Plan (Wireless Adapter Settings -> Power Saving Mode = Maximum Performance) also mitigated.
- On the controller side, reducing FT roam aggressiveness and increasing reassociation hold time reduced the frequency but did not fully eliminate.
- Packet captures show keepalives delayed by up to 90s during roam; WireGuard handshake resumes but application sockets experience default TCP timeouts.
- Intune profile was used to push temporary registry settings and wireless power plan to a pilot group (10 machines) with success.

Mitigation applied:
- Deployed Intune device configuration to pilot cohort (10 users in SF) to set Wireless Adapter power to Maximum Performance and to disable the 'allow device to sleep' option.
- Rolled controller config change to throttle FT handoffs to be less aggressive (increase reassociation timer to 500ms).
- Sent bug report to Intel with packet captures and driver logs; vendor acknowledged potential driver-side race and requested additional telemetry.

Next steps / plan:
- Expand Intune mitigation to all SF office laptops (scheduled phased rollout over 3 days).
- Validate Intel driver fix when available; test in lab (matrix: Windows builds x driver versions x WG config).
- Add a customer-side guard in redwood-edge agent: detect roaming handshake gap > 10s and force reassertion of wg route metrics (work queued as GH PR).
- Update wireless runbook with mitigation steps and add monitoring rule for 'wg handshake gap' on fleet.

Related artifacts and links:
- Packet captures: s3://redwood-captures/INT-864209/roam-pcap-0309.pcap (internal)
- Controller config change PR: https://git.internal.redwood.net/network/configs/pull/122
comments: 2026-03-10 09:18 - Diego Morales (on-call SRE): Confirmed tcpdump from gateway shows keepalive gap matching user's reported timeouts. Asked for OS and driver versions.
2026-03-10 10:02 - Aisha Patel (Reporter): Provided two affected machine logs and Intune device ID list for SF pilot. Attached Event Viewer WLAN logs.
2026-03-11 14:37 - Ethan Park (Assignee): Reproduced locally with AX211 driver 22.50.1. Temporary mitigation (power settings) removed the issue. Pushing Intune profile to 10 pilot machines now.
2026-03-12 08:55 - Network Eng (Marta Kim): Controller FT timers adjusted; saw reduced roam frequency but not a full fix. Suggest we wait for vendor driver fix and keep mitigations in place.
2026-03-13 16:20 - Security (Rajiv Sinha): Confirmed Intune changes do not change firewall posture or keys. OK to proceed to wider rollout after test window.
2026-03-14 09:10 - Ethan Park: Pilot rollout successful. No recurrences on pilot machines in 48h. Preparing phased rollout to SF fleet 3/15-3/17. Pending Intel driver verification.
source.investigation_notes: Key findings: driver queues during DTIM handoff; WireGuard keepalives delayed; Windows route recalculation introduces lower-metric gateway route before wg routes reassert. Temporary device-level power profile fix works; network controller tuning reduces frequency. Next: agent-level guard and vendor driver fix. Refer to controller logs and wg handshake graphs in the packet capture directory.
source.resolution_notes: Mitigations: Intune policy (wireless adapter power = Maximum Performance, disable 'allow device to sleep') deployed to pilot and now expanded to full SF office. Controller FT timers adjusted. Long-term: validate Intel driver fix and merge agent guard (GH PR linked). Will mark Resolved after successful validation of vendor driver across 2-week window.
follow_up_actions: Expand Intune rollout to SF office (scheduled 2026-03-15 to 2026-03-17)
Validate new Intel driver in test matrix and report results
Implement agent-level route reassert guard (PR linked)
