source.summary: Intermittent path MTU blackhole with 464XLAT/CLAT causing VPN tunnel TLS stalls to internal k8s API
source.description:
Issue summary: Several on-prem and remote employees intermittently lose connectivity to internal Kubernetes control-plane endpoints (k8s API) over the corporate VPN. Symptoms include HTTPS stalls (TLS connection hung after ClientHello/ServerHello), kubectl timeouts, and SSO failures when Okta redirects attempt to hit cluster endpoints.

Impact: Affects ~8% of remote users on IPv6-only mobile hotspots and some office users routed through a branch edge running CLAT/464XLAT for IPv6-only carrier links. Breaks developer workflows (kubectl, code pushes) and automated jobs that depend on k8s API. No evidence of a serving platform outage; problem correlates with path MTU size and IPv6-to-IPv4 translation path.

Environment: Production, corporate VPN (WireGuard-based branch/host tunnels), edge NAT64/464XLAT devices at two branch sites (boston-edge, seattle-edge). Affected clients: macOS 14 and Android hotspots. Control-plane endpoint IP: 10.20.5.12 (internal VPC).

Observed behavior: TLS handshakes appear to complete in some packet captures but subsequent HTTP traffic stalls. tracepath/traceroute6 show MTU clamping on the path when CLAT is present; larger packets (1480+) are dropped without ICMPv6 Packet Too Big messages being delivered to the client. The VPN uses UDP encapsulation and fragmentation is disabled on clients (df set).

Expected behavior: PMTU discovery should work across translated paths. Larger packets should either be fragmented/PMTU-adjusted or ICMPv6/PTB should be forwarded so endpoints can reduce MSS and avoid stalls.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Connect a macOS laptop to mobile carrier hotspot configured IPv6-only (carrier uses 464XLAT/CLAT on the CPE).
2) Establish WireGuard VPN to prod-us-east via corporate profile (UDP encapsulation).
3) Run 'kubectl get pods --all-namespaces' against internal k8s API (10.20.5.12) or curl https://10.20.5.12/api. Request may hang after TLS setup.
4) Run 'sudo tracepath -n 10.20.5.12' and 'tcpdump -i any icmp6 or icmp' on laptop and edge to observe PTB behavior.
5) Toggle client MTU to 1280 and re-run requests (observe workaround success).
logs: pcap_excerpt: client->server TLS ClientHello (len 512), server->client ServerHello (len 256), subsequent TCP segments with MSS 1460 never ACKed in capture window.
tcpdump_edge_boston: outgoing IPv6 packets fragmented/clamped; no ICMPv6 PTB forwarded back to client (filtered by carrier NAT).
tracepath_output: pmtu 1280 detected between client and first-hop but path through CLAT to internal 10.20.5.12 requires lower MTU; intermediate hop dropped 1420 packets without PTB.
vpn_logs: wg-quick reports peer handshake OK; UDP session active; no peer disconnects.
system_logs: macOS kernel shows 'IPv6: packet too big' entries but application sockets never reduce MSS because PTB not delivered to user-space due to encapsulation/iptables rules.
investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: PMTUD failure due to IPv6-to-IPv4 translation (464XLAT) on carrier/edge resulting in PTB dropped by carrier NAT or not propagated back through WireGuard UDP encapsulation.
Verified reproducible with IPv6-only carrier hotspot and with branch edge using CLAT (seattle-edge). Non-IPv6 clients on standard NATed IPv4 home links do not see the issue.
Workaround: forcing client MTU to 1280 or enabling fragmentation (client-side) allows traffic to flow and kubectl resumes. This reduces throughput but restores connectivity.

Checked iptables/nft rules on branch edge: we have MASQUERADE rules but no explicit ICMPv6 handling; WireGuard endpoint does not decapsulate/rewrap ICMPv6 in a way that Docker/k8s control-plane notices.

Captured pcap on edge shows ICMPv6 PTB generated by internal path but carrier's NAT64 implementation strips or rate-limits PTB messages and they never reach the client; therefore PMTUD cannot complete.
Reproduced in lab using an IPv6-only test SIM and vendor provided CLAT appliance (replicates behavior).
comments: 2026-03-12 09:24 - Ava Chen: Created ticket after first customer-facing report from devs in #support. Initial triage indicates MTU-related failure on IPv6-only clients.
2026-03-12 10:05 - Diego Martinez: Assigned for deeper network trace. Will capture pcap at edge and client. Asked for list of affected users and common carriers.
2026-03-12 13:42 - NetOps: Captured pcap on boston-edge; saw PTB generated by internal router but not delivered upstream to carrier. Suspect CLAT box rewriting ICMP or carrier filtering.
2026-03-13 08:18 - Diego Martinez: Lab reproduction succeeded with vendor CLAT appliance. Created GitHub issue for edge NAT64 behavior and PR to add ICMP passthrough rules for WireGuard interface.
2026-03-13 15:06 - SRE: Noted that enabling 'df' clear on client allowed fragmentation by WireGuard and restored traffic; that's a temporary mitigation if client-side change possible.
2026-03-14 09:52 - Diego Martinez: Engaging carrier support for IPv6 CLAT behavior; filed support ticket with vendor for CLAT appliance to confirm ICMPv6 handling and PTB translation semantics.
2026-03-14 12:30 - Security: Reviewed workaround and confirmed no policy violation to temporarily enable fragmentation for affected support users.

2026-03-14 14:10 - Diego Martinez: Proposed multi-pronged mitigation: 1) Add WireGuard postrouting rules to translate/forward ICMPv6 PTB into ICMPv4 PTB, 2) Short-term client MTU profile of 1280 pushed to mobile dev group via MDM, 3) Carrier vendor escalation.
source.root_cause: End-to-end PMTUD failed because ICMPv6 'Packet Too Big' messages were lost/filtered across the CLAT/NAT64 translation boundary and not propagated through the UDP-encapsulated WireGuard tunnel back to the IPv6-only client. The result is a silent path MTU blackhole for larger TLS payloads.
source.resolution:
Workarounds applied: pushed client MTU profile (1280) to affected MDM group and enabled WireGuard interface iptables rule to allow/forward ICMPv6/PTB translations for known branch edges. Ongoing: vendor engagement to fix CLAT appliance ICMP translation semantics and plan for permanent fix in infra to translate PTB across encapsulation. Rolling change in staging scheduled to add ICMP translation rule and small MTU enforcement on VPN egress.

Follow-ups: 1) Postmortem to document PMTUD checks in runbook. 2) Add automated synthetic tests for IPv6-only path MTU to internal CI. 3) Coordinate with carrier/vendor to ensure PTB propagation.
next_steps: 1) Implement WireGuard iptables/nft rule in staging to map ICMPv6 PTB <-> ICMPv4 PTB and test with lab CLAT appliance (owner: Diego, ETA: 2026-03-18).
2) Push temporary MTU 1280 profile to affected users via MDM (owner: IT Ops, ETA: 2026-03-15).
3) Open vendor escalation with CLAT/Carrier (owner: NetOps, ticket ref: VND-2211).
4) Add postmortem and update network runbook with checks for IPv6 PMTUD (owner: SRE, ETA: 2026-03-22).

title: Intermittent path MTU blackhole with 464XLAT/CLAT causing VPN tunnel TLS stalls to internal k8s API
preview:
Issue summary: Several on-prem and remote employees intermittently lose connectivity to internal Kubernetes control-plane endpoints (k8s API) over the corporate VPN. Symptoms include HTTPS stalls (TLS connection hung after ClientHello/ServerHello), kubectl timeouts, and SSO failures when Okta redirects attempt to hit cluster endpoints.

Impact: Affects ~8% of remote users on IPv6-only mobile hotspots and some office users routed through a branch edge running CLAT/464XLAT for IPv6-only carrier links. Breaks developer workflows (kubectl, code pushes) and automated jobs that depend on k8s API. No evidence of a serving platform outage; problem correlates with path MTU size and IPv6-to-IPv4 translation path.

Environment: Production, corporate VPN (WireGuard-based branch/host tunnels), edge NAT64/464XLAT devices at two branch sites (boston-edge, seattle-edge). Affected clients: macOS 14 and Android hotspots. Control-plane endpoint IP: 10.20.5.12 (internal VPC).

Observed behavior: TLS handshakes appear to complete in some packet captures but subsequent HTTP traffic stalls. tracepath/traceroute6 show MTU clamping on the path when CLAT is present; larger packets (1480+) are dropped without ICMPv6 Packet Too Big messages being delivered to the client. The VPN uses UDP encapsulation and fragmentation is disabled on clients (df set).

Expected behavior: PMTU discovery should work across translated paths. Larger packets should either be fragmented/PMTU-adjusted or ICMPv6/PTB should be forwarded so endpoints can reduce MSS and avoid stalls.

Impact: Affects ~8% of remote users on IPv6-only mobile hotspots and some office users routed through a branch edge running CLAT/464XLAT for IPv6-only carrier links. The VPN uses UDP encapsulation and fragmentation is disabled on clients (df set).

Expected behavior: PMTU discovery should work across translated paths. Larger packets should either be fragmented/PMTU-adjusted or ICMPv6/PTB should be forwarded so endpoints can reduce MSS and avoid stalls.

source.summary: Phased offboarding for senior engineer: partial SSO deprovision delay, expiring bridge service-account, and corporate mobile return logistics
source.description:
Issue summary: Senior SWE (employee# 92014) is transitioning from full-time to a 6-week contractor bridge starting 2026-03-15. HR requests a phased offboarding rather than immediate deprovisioning because the engineer will complete two critical rollups: (1) a code-owner handoff for the inference scheduler repo and (2) a multi-region migration playbook.

Impact: Immediate full deprovisioning would block final rollouts and risk a regression in production. Delaying some access increases risk surface and requires controlled, auditable exceptions (temporary service account + expiring keys).

Request: 1) Keep SSO and repo write access for two weeks (until 2026-03-29) but remove access to billing consoles and dedicated GPU lab immediately. 2) Create a temporary bridge service-account scoped to the inference-scheduler repo and the release pipeline with keys that expire 2026-03-29. 3) Reassign code-owner and create a PR for ownership changes before 2026-03-18. 4) Coordinate physical asset return: company phone must be shipped back to UK office (return window 2026-03-20 to 2026-03-24) and laptop to be couriered to HQ. 5) Security/Legal sign-off required for exception; provide audit trail.

Environment: prod (eu-west), infra components touched: IAM, KMS, GitHub, asset-inventory, courier vendor (DHL), HR caseworker queue.

Policy question: Does Redwood allow a timeboxed SSO delay with a scoped, expiring service account for a contractor bridge? What approving authority is required and which retention/audit records are mandatory for the exception?
steps_to_reproduce: N/A - HR workflow / access policy question rather than system bug
investigation_notes: 2026-03-12 09:10 (Miguel): Reviewed employee access lists; user has org-admin on one repo and write on two others. Billing console access detected and proposed for immediate removal.

2026-03-12 11:45 (Security): Temporary service-account pattern approved in principle if scoped and key lifecycle is enforced (KMS rotate + audit). Requires Legal sign-off because contract status is changing.
2026-03-13 08:30 (HR - Emma Li): Contract conversion paperwork has been started; return window for phone confirmed with employee (preferred to drop at UK office or ship).
2026-03-13 14:05 (Dev Lead - Priya Patel): Will open ownership PR today; will accept patch for handover and tag backups in repo. Suggest limiting bridge SA to release/pipeline scope only.
comments: Ava Chen (Reporter) - 2026-03-12: Raised with HR after exit interview. Please advise on minimum safe access to allow release completion.
Miguel Torres (Assignee) - 2026-03-12: I will remove billing console access by EOD and draft a temporary service-account spec for Security review.
Security (Olga Novak) - 2026-03-12: Temporary SA pattern OK but require KMS envelope encryption and time-limited keys; send specifics.
Legal (Daniel Kline) - 2026-03-13: Need executed contractor amendment before any access exception; can process expeditiously if HR confirms timeline.
Priya Patel (Dev Lead) - 2026-03-13: Opening PR for code-owner handoff: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/1428. Will merge after 48h review or sooner if tests pass.
HR (Emma Li) - 2026-03-13: Employee agreed to ship phone back on 2026-03-20. Please confirm courier label and asset tag checklists.
actions_taken: 2026-03-12: Billing console access revoked (confirmed) - actioned by Miguel.
2026-03-12: Flagged dedicated GPU lab access for immediate suspension pending manager confirmation.
2026-03-13: Drafted temp service-account scope and TTL (2026-03-29) and submitted to Security for sign-off.
2026-03-13: Asset return label request submitted to Office Ops for DHL pickup from employee address on 2026-03-20.
next_steps: Security to provide final SA spec and KMS policy (due 2026-03-14).
Legal to confirm contractor amendment is received (HR to attach to ticket).
Miguel to provision temp SA with TTL after Security+Legal sign-off (target 2026-03-15).
Dev lead to merge ownership PR and update CODEOWNERS by 2026-03-18.
Office Ops to confirm courier pickup and provide tracking by 2026-03-19.
resolution: Pending - awaiting Security and Legal sign-off; temporary mitigations applied (billing access removed).

source.summary: Microphone fails to enumerate after BIOS flash + Secure Boot toggle during depot repair
source.description:
Issue summary: After a standard depot BIOS flash followed by toggling Secure Boot to re-enable legacy imaging paths, attached device microphones (internal array) no longer enumerate in Windows. Observed on small subset of fleet following recent vendor BIOS (v1.18.4) rollout to depot units.

Impact: Affected users see "No audio input device installed" in Settings and Device Manager; internal microphone missing from audio input list. External USB microphones still function. This blocks users from joining voice calls and impacts QA signoff for a subset of refurbished laptops. Approximately 7 confirmed units to date, all processed through the same depot workflow.

Environment: Depot repair/flash workflow, corp provisioning image (Win11-23H2-corp), Secure Boot toggled from Enabled -> Disabled -> Enabled during repair validation; BIOS version 1.18.4 on affected units.

Observed behavior: After reboot, Windows audio subsystem loads but Microphone Array device does not appear in Device Manager; no driver nodes for the audio DSP. Windows Event Log shows Service Control Manager entries for audioendpointbuilder failing to start dependent driver with Code 7000. Kernel logs show ACPI _DSM calls returning different hardware IDs after Secure Boot toggle.

Expected behavior: Internal microphone enumerates and appropriate audio codec driver (vendor) is present under Sound, video and game controllers and Audio inputs and outputs.

Notes: This ticket is an internal support/depot-repair workflow issue; not customer-facing. Triage requested to determine whether the BIOS change altered firmware visibility or audio codec microcontroller (DSP) firmware got re-flashed/disabled when Secure Boot state changed.
source.steps_to_reproduce:
1) Take affected model (Lenovo X1 Gen10 / Dell 7440) with BIOS v1.18.4.
2) Run depot BIOS flash script (vendor recommended image).
3) During imaging validate, toggle Secure Boot: Enabled -> Disabled -> Enabled (this is part of depot validation for legacy imaging fallback).
4) Boot into WinPE, apply corp image and driver pack, complete OOBE and restart.
5) Log in and open Settings > System > Sound and Device Manager > Audio inputs and outputs.
6) Observe missing internal microphone; external USB mic enumerates.
7) Collect Windows Event Logs (System/Application) and run 'pnputil /enum-devices' and 'driverquery /v'.
source.logs:
System event: Service Control Manager 7000 - The AudioEndpointBuilder service failed to start due to the following error: The driver failed to load.
Kernel: ACPI: _DSM returned HWID mismatch for HDAUDIO device after Secure Boot toggle (trace id: acpi-evt-20260310-01).
Device manager output: No 'Microphone Array' under 'Audio inputs and outputs'.
Driver pack installer log: vendor-audio-driver.inf skipped: signature validation failed (policy: secureboot-recheck).
investigation_notes: 03/10 Aisha: Initial triage at depot - reproduced on 2/7 units processed this morning. Verified BIOS version and image steps; Secure Boot toggled per depot checklist.

03/11 Ethan: Collected Event Logs and performed driver pack install manually. Observed INF skipped due to signature validation when Secure Boot state set to Enabled at end of workflow. When Secure Boot is left Disabled, driver installs and microphone appears—indicates SB interplay with vendor driver signing or firmware validation.

03/11 Hardware Tech (Maya): Dumped audio DSP firmware via vendor tool on affected unit; firmware blob present but manifest signature mismatch reported after BIOS flash + SB toggle sequence. Vendor firmware component appears intact but bootloader rejects it when SB re-enabled without vendor key/manifest present.

03/12 Eng (Ravi): Compared BIOS release notes; 1.18.4 adds an 'audio codec lockdown' feature that enforces signature verification of codec firmware if Secure Boot is Enabled. Our depot workflow toggles SB, which causes the pre-boot manifest to be rewritten by vendor tool under Disabled state; re-enabling SB makes the manifest fail signature checks.
comments: Aisha Malik (2026-03-10): Created ticket after depot tech reported 2 units. Assigned to Ethan for deep-dive.
Ethan Park (2026-03-11): Reproduced locally in lab. Workaround: keep Secure Boot Disabled until vendor-signed driver/manifest installed. Need to avoid leaving SB toggled in depot workflow.

Maya Chen - Depot Tech (2026-03-11): Confirmed vendor tool can reflash codec firmware if bootloader unlocked; process takes ~6 minutes per unit. Requires vendor-provided signed manifest to pass SB validation.
Ravi Patel - Firmware (2026-03-12): Reached out to vendor support thread; vendor acknowledged change and provided internal ticket RDL-4621. Waiting on signed manifest and updated driver pack.
resolution_notes: Mitigation (short-term): Depot procedure updated to NOT re-enable Secure Boot until after vendor-signed driver pack and codec manifest are installed. Depot checklist version bumped to 2026-03-12_v2. Deployed a temporary step in SCCM task sequence to force-install vendor driver when SB is Disabled.
Patch plan (medium-term): Vendor to deliver signed codec manifest + updated driver pack. Eng to validate on staging images and then push driver pack to driver-packs repo (PR referenced).

Follow-ups: 1) Add a pre-imaging validation that detects BIOS 1.18.4 and flags units for 'codec manifest restore' step. 2) Add automation to depot telemetry to capture microphone enumeration status post-imaging. 3) Post-mortem with vendor and update runbook (confluence page) to include this signature interplay.

source.summary: UK BACS payment rejected: beneficiary address missing on vendor bank record causing payment hold
source.description:
Issue summary:
A scheduled UK BACS payment to vendor 'Northbridge Components Ltd' was rejected by the bank with return code RM124 (beneficiary address missing). Payment was submitted on 2026-03-09 as part of the weekly AP run and entered 'Rejected by bank' status in the payments ledger.

Impact:
- Vendor invoice for PO PO-99877 (net £40,000) plus VAT remains unpaid; vendor has flagged potential shipment hold if not cleared within 3 business days.
- Affects one high-value supplier on monthly cadence.
- Treasury team unable to reprocess until beneficiary details are updated in vendor master and bank resubmission performed.

Environment:
- Payments system: Payments Portal v3.4 (production)
- Bank channel: UK BACS via Banking Partner (Clearline Payments)
- Region: eu-west-2 (London)

Steps to reproduce / observed sequence:
1) AP run triggered 2026-03-09 02:00 UTC for UK suppliers.
2) Payments Portal generated BACS file and transmitted to Clearline.
3) Bank acknowledged receipt, then returned the transaction with RM124.
4) Payments Portal logged rejection and flagged payment for manual intervention.

Attachments / Logs:
- Payments Portal audit trail ID: pay-20260309-99877
- Bank return message (Clearline): "RM124 - Beneficiary address incomplete/missing" (see attached bank XML in ticket attachments).

Requested action:
- Confirm missing beneficiary address fields and update vendor master record.
- Coordinate with treasury to reissue payment (manual or batch amend) and confirm routing.
- Notify vendor of expected reissue time and confirm no shipment interruption.
investigation_notes: 2026-03-10 09:15 - Diego: Retrieved bank return file from Clearline; RM124 corresponds to missing beneficiary address line or postcode. Bank requires full address for BACS on merchant record for certain banks.

2026-03-10 10:00 - Payments team: Cross-checked vendor master (Payments Portal) -> vendor record shows only 'City' and 'Country' populated; address lines 1-3 empty. NetSuite supplier record has full address, but mapping layer between NetSuite -> Payments Portal missed the address fields on last sync (sync job ns-sync-03 failed on 2026-03-08).

2026-03-10 11:20 - Treasury: Confirmed Clearline cannot accept amendment via automated file; requires new instruction with beneficiary address included. Recommended manual reissue via portal after vendor record update.

2026-03-11 14:05 - AP: Reached out to vendor (AP contact Jane Foster) to confirm full legal bank address and beneficiary name spelling. Vendor provided corrected address and confirmed no change to account number or sort code.
Hypothesis: Missing address fields introduced by NS-sync job failure; downstream mapping left blank which triggered bank-side validation and rejection.

Next steps: 1) Patch vendor master in Payments Portal with address lines provided by vendor. 2) Re-run validation in staging for one test payment. 3) Treasury to manually reissue BACS instruction and confirm acceptance by Clearline. 4) Fix the NS-sync job and backfill any vendors affected in last 7 days.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:30 - Ava Chen (Reporter): Noted vendor called CS-AP escalation complaining about shipment hold. Marking as P1 due to potential operational impact.
2026-03-10 11:45 - Diego Martinez (Assignee): Pulled bank file; attaching bank return XML. Looping in @Priya (Payments Integrations) and @Liam (Treasury).

2026-03-10 12:10 - Priya Rao (Payments Integrations): ns-sync-03 had error 'mapping:address_line1 missing' on 2026-03-08 at 22:04 UTC. Investigating root cause; likely schema change in NetSuite export.
2026-03-11 15:00 - Liam O'Connor (Treasury): Manual reissue ready pending address update. Please confirm vendor-provided address matches KYC doc before resubmitting.
2026-03-12 08:20 - AP (Jane Foster, vendor): Confirming address: 'Unit 4, Riverside Park, Old Mill Road, Reading, RG1 8XY, United Kingdom'. Please advise when payment will be resent.
2026-03-13 09:05 - Diego Martinez: Updated Payments Portal vendor master with address and revalidated record. Treasury to send manual instruction today and confirm clearline acceptance.
source.resolution: Workaround in progress: vendor master updated with full beneficiary address; treasury preparing manual BACS reissue. Long-term fix: remediate ns-sync-03 mapping error and add automated schema validation for vendor address fields. Vendor notified and shipment team copied. Will update ticket when bank confirms acceptance.

source.summary: Cleanup and reconcile SLA orchestration: template variables, routing keys, and reporting parity
source.description:
Issue summary:
Recent deploys to sla-enforcer-v2 and the rule-engine refactor created several small divergences between SLA configuration, routing key normalization, and the downstream reporting transforms. The net effect: daily SLA breach counts in the support dashboard are ~7% lower than expected and some internal routing macros do not expand, causing tickets to be auto-assigned to the default queue instead of tiered on-call.

Impact:
- Incorrect auto-assignment for ~120 internal support tickets last week (internal queues).
- Daily SLA breach totals for internal dashboards are undercounted (~7% gap) which affects SRE on-call capacity planning.
- Risk of misrouted P1/P2 escalations if macros are not fixed before next release window.

Observed behavior / Evidence:
- Audit log from sla-enforcer (2026-03-08 03:14 - 03:46 UTC) shows macro expansion failures: variable "priority_level" not found; templates expect "priority".
- Rule-engine PR #1420 changed routing key normalization from kebab-case to snake_case; existing SLA configs reference kebab-case route keys (e.g., "sla-high-latency").
- Reporting pipeline transform (support-alerts consumer) drop observed for field "sla_target_ms" after schema evolution on Kafka topic support.alerts.v2 (see consumer offset gaps and schema registry diff).
- Slack thread: #internal-sla-tooling (2026-03-09) contains a reproduction trace and raw event samples.

Hypothesis / Root cause (working):
1) Template variable rename: a configuration migration in sla-enforcer-v2 introduced a canonical variable name "priority_level" while existing macros and templates used "priority"; the templating layer does not provide a backwards-compatibility alias.
2) Routing key normalization mismatch: rule-engine normalization change means configured routing rules no longer match incoming normalized keys unless configs are migrated.
3) Reporting transform schema drift: reporting consumer expects legacy schema and silently drops fields it doesn't find, producing undercounted metrics.

Immediate mitigation (short-term):
- Revert sla-enforcer template parsing to accept both "priority" and "priority_level" (hotfix branch prepared: support-tooling PR #1432).
- Add temporary adapter in rule-engine that canonicalizes incoming keys to both formats to avoid loss of matches (feature flag controlled).
- Patch reporting consumer to log dropped fields and emit counts to metrics for visibility while consumer is migrated.

Proposed long-term fix (plan):
- Full config migration script that rewrites existing routing keys to snake_case and updates macros to canonical variable names; add migration dry-run and rollback support.
- Add schema/version headers to support.alerts topics and upgrade reporting consumers with explicit schema evolution handling; add an alert when "sla_target_ms" is missing from >0.1% of messages.
- Add unit and integration tests that exercise macro expansion for both legacy and new templates and include these checks in the release pipeline.

Acceptance criteria:
- Macro expansion error rate drops to <0.01% for the next 7 days in prod.
- Routing miss rate due to key normalization drops to zero after migration (validated by matched/unmatched routing metrics).
- Daily SLA counts in dashboard reconcile to within 0.5% of ground-truth sampling (manual audit) for two consecutive days.

Next steps / Actions assigned:
- Mateo Alvarez: apply hotfix to sla-enforcer and enable feature flag for adapter in rule-engine (ETA 2026-03-12).
- Priya Mehta (Reporting): deploy patch to reporting consumer to surface dropped-field metrics and run backfill on 2026-03-11's topic window.
- Leah Patel: author config migration script and schedule maintenance window; create rollback plan and confluence doc.

Follow-ups / Observability playbook additions:
- Add a dedicated Grafana panel for template expansion error rate and quota for the support team.
- Create a runbook step to run the routing-key migration dry-run before any rule-engine normalization changes.
- Add a pre-deploy integration test that validates that the support console resolves macros against a canonical template set.

Priority and timeline rationale:
This is P2/Sev2 because the issue affects internal routing and metrics accuracy (operational visibility), but there is a straightforward hotfix and low immediate customer impact. The goal is to complete migration and validation before the next monthly deploy cycle.

2026-03-10 11:03 - Priya Mehta: Reporting patch submitted (PR #710). Patch adds logging and a Prometheus counter for dropped fields. Running local backfill against test topic; will schedule prod backfill after hotfix rollouts.

title: Cleanup and reconcile SLA orchestration: template variables, routing keys, and reporting parity
preview:

source.summary: Runner init fails: kubeconfig file mode changed by secrets-sync job, preventing runner startup for on-prem release fleet
source.description:
Issue summary:
During the nightly secrets reconciliation (secrets-sync) we observed that the kubeconfig used by our self-hosted release runners in the on-prem release cluster had its file mode changed from 0640 to 0600. The runner systemd unit runs as user 'runner' and group 'ci', and relies on group-read to access /etc/runner/kubeconfig. After the mode change runners fail to start, fail health checks, and cannot pick up deployment jobs.

Impact:
- Nightly release jobs for several teams (release/* pipelines) failed to start between 2026-03-10 02:10 and 2026-03-10 03:40 UTC.
- ~22 CI jobs were queued and timed out, delaying a cross-functional canary promotion.
- No user-facing production outages, but release cadence blocked.

Environment:
- prod on-prem release cluster in our us-west colo.
- Runner fleet: self-hosted systemd runners (image: redwood/runner:2026.02.1).
- Secrets pipeline: ansible-driven secrets-sync job that runs hourly and re-applies kubeconfigs and vault-wrapped secrets.

Steps to reproduce:
1) On a runner host, inspect /etc/runner/kubeconfig file mode and ownership (expected: -rw-r----- runner:ci).
2) Trigger secrets-sync job with playbook 'deploy/k8s/kubeconfigs.yml' against release hosts.
3) Observe resulting file mode changed to -rw------- and systemd-runner fails to start with permission errors.

Observed behavior / logs:
- systemd journal from runner host: "src/runtime/init: failed to open kubeconfig /etc/runner/kubeconfig: permission denied"
- /var/log/messages: "Runner process (pid 4321) permission denied reading /etc/runner/kubeconfig"
- secrets-sync ansible task shows: 'copy: mode=0600 owner=root group=root dest=/etc/runner/kubeconfig' (playbook currently defaults to root:root/0600 when re-rendering templates)

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis: recent change in the secrets-sync playbook (PR #3746) introduced a conservative file mode default (0600) for generated kubeconfigs; previously we applied 0640 with group 'ci'. The change was intended for security but didn't account for the runner service user model on on-prem hosts.
- Confirmed by reproducing the playbook run in staging: playbook sets mode=0600 and owner=root:root.
- Runners created after 2026-02-20 use a different startup script that drops privileges to runner:ci; older hosts had kubeconfigs managed by a different mechanism and were unaffected. The mix of host generation templates caused an inconsistent fleet state.
- No vault/KMS errors were observed; this is strictly a file-permissions regression from the deployment step.

Mitigation applied:
- Rollback: temporarily reverted deploy/k8s/kubeconfigs.yml change in secrets-sync (rolled back PR #3746 in the orchestration repo) at 2026-03-10 03:05 UTC.
- Escalated hotfix: executed a remediation ansible run to restore file mode and ownership across runner hosts: mode=0640 owner=root group=ci for /etc/runner/kubeconfig and restarted systemd runner service.
- Restarted ~120 runners; ~96 returned healthy within 7 minutes. Remaining hosts had unrelated disk pressure and require separate tickets.

Current status:
- Runners are recovering; release queue processing resumed at 2026-03-10 03:40 UTC.
- Change reverted in secrets-sync, and a targeted remediation played across prod fleet completed.

Open questions and next steps:
1) Modify secrets-sync playbook to accept per-host ACL parameters and to set group 'ci' and 0640 mode for runner kubeconfig artifacts.
2) Add a pre-flight validation in the runner systemd unit that emits a clear metric/count when kubeconfig permissions are non-compliant so alarms trigger before job failures.
3) Add a CI gate that runs a lint/validation on secrets playbooks for file mode changes that could break services.
4) Perform an audit to find any other services relying on group-read for generated secrets.

Owner for remediation and follow-ups: Priya Nair (Release Eng) in collaboration with Secrets Ops (Diego Morales).

Related artifacts: see linked PRs and runbooks below.
comments: 2026-03-10 02:50 - Diego Morales: Noted spike in job failures; initial logs show permission denied reading kubeconfig. Suspect secrets-sync run around 02:07 UTC.
2026-03-10 02:58 - Priya Nair: Started targeted remediation ansible run to reset file modes and restart runner.service. Rolling restart across on-prem hosts in batches of 20.
2026-03-10 03:12 - Ava Chen: Confirmed rollback of PR #3746 in orchestration repo. Will create follow-up to implement per-host ACLs.
2026-03-10 03:45 - Priya Nair: Most runners healthy; releasing queued jobs. Will monitor for 24h and then propose playbook change in Secrets Ops retro.
2026-03-12 09:20 - Diego Morales: Added acceptance test to orchestration repo that verifies kubeconfig ownership/mode for runner hosts during CI. PR #3762 references this ticket.
follow_up_tasks: Create playbook param for kubeconfig_mode and kubeconfig_group
Add systemd preflight permission check + alert
Audit other generated secrets for group-read assumptions

source.summary: Possible SSO session replay after employee clicked external form link
source.description:
Issue summary: An employee clicked a link in an externally hosted vendor form received via email. Within one hour, anomalous session activity for that account was observed: short-lived logins from unfamiliar IPs and requests that matched session-reuse patterns. Impact: potential session token exposure and attempted session replay. Initial scope: single user account (product-ops@redwood.ai), possibly more if the external form captured session info via query params or third-party trackers.

Timeline:
- 2026-03-12 09:27 UTC: Employee reports odd redirect after clicking vendor form link (Support Slack #sec-alerts).
- 2026-03-12 09:34 UTC: Proxy logs show outbound request to analytics.example-forms.com with a query string including a transient token-like value.
- 2026-03-12 09:45 UTC: Cloudflare auth logs show additional login events for product-ops@redwood.ai from two foreign IPs (AS12345) within a 10-minute window.
- 2026-03-12 10:10 UTC: Security detection flagged multiple short-lived sessions and created this ticket.

Environment: Production Redwood Console, us-east region, SSO via Okta (OIDC), corporate proxy (BlueGate) filtering egress.

Steps to reproduce (initial hypothesis):
1) User opens vendor email and clicks link to external hosted form (vendor domain: forms-vendor.com).
2) The hosted form includes third-party analytics (analytics.example-forms.com) which captures URL parameters.
3) A session identifier was appended to the redirect URL (possibly from the browser referrer or an incorrectly constructed omniauth return URL) and ended up in the analytics request.
4) Remote actor(s) replayed captured token, attempting to re-use the session against our auth endpoint.

Logs / evidence references:
- BlueGate proxy request: outbound GET to https://analytics.example-forms.com/collect?v=1&sid=eyJhbGci... (truncated) timestamp 2026-03-12T09:34:02Z (see proxy trace ID BG-8813).
- Cloudflare auth logs: login events for product-ops@redwood.ai from 185.62.44.10 and 185.62.44.11 at 09:45 UTC (location: offshore region); user agent: curl/7.85.0.
- Okta sessions API: session token for product-ops account was active from 09:25-09:48 UTC and then invalidated at 10:05 UTC.

Initial triage: Presumed session token leaked via URL parameter captured by third-party analytics. No evidence yet of privilege escalation or data exfil beyond attempted session reuse. We have temporarily revoked the user sessions and rotated API service tokens for the user's active ephemeral keys.
investigation_notes: 09:40 - Security: pulled proxy BG logs; matched URL parameter 'sid' that resembles Okta session JWT pattern (header.payload.signature format).
09:52 - Infra: cross-checked Cloudflare: source IPs map to known bulletproof hosting ASN; timestamps align with analytics capture time plus ~10 minutes for replay attempts.
10:05 - Identity: Okta reports session invalidation event after admin-initiated revoke (admin: miguel.ortiz@redwood.ai).
10:20 - Forensics: no sign of lateral authentication from the compromised account to internal services other than console login attempts; no S3 or internal API access observed from those foreign IPs.
10:45 - Network: blocked analytics.example-forms.com at the proxy and added domain to denylist across corp egress rules; temporarily blocked AS12345 at border firewall.
11:15 - User outreach: product-ops@redwood.ai completed password reset and re-enrolled MFA; user confirms only clicked the single link and did not paste tokens anywhere.
12:00 - Evidence snapshot: exported proxy trace BG-8813, cloudflare logs slice CF-3312, Okta session dump OKT-546 for security audit.

Investigation open items: determine whether other users visited same vendor form URL and whether any browser extensions may have caused URL leakage; check vendor form configuration for third-party trackers and misconfigured redirection parameters.
comments: Ava Chen (reporter) - 2026-03-12 09:30: Reported by product-ops user in #sec-alerts. User says link redirected to a long URL and then to form; felt 'weird' and reported immediately.
Miguel Ortiz (assignee) - 2026-03-12 09:50: Revoked Okta sessions for product-ops user and rotated short-lived tokens tied to CLI. Starting egress block for analytics.example-forms.com.

SRE - 2026-03-12 10:00: Confirmed two failed token reuse attempts from 185.62.44.10/11 with user agent curl; attempts hit /api/console/session-check endpoint and returned 401 after session invalidation.
Security Ops - 2026-03-12 10:30: Placing domain on proxy denylist and contacting vendor security contact to request form audit; shared indicators with Threat Intel (ASN, IPs, suspicious UA).
Product Ops (affected user) - 2026-03-12 11:20: Completed forced password reset and MFA re-enroll; reports no suspicious changes to account settings.

Miguel Ortiz - 2026-03-12 12:05: Created temporary runbook for similar events: 1) revoke sessions, 2) rotate tokens, 3) denylist domain, 4) notify vendor, 5) audit other users. Need to formalize as playbook.
resolution_notes: Short-term mitigations applied: revoked active Okta sessions for affected account, rotated ephemeral API keys linked to product-ops user, applied proxy denylist for analytics.example-forms.com, and blocked offending ASN at edge for three hours.
User remediation: affected user changed password and re-enrolled MFA; guidance sent to all employees advising caution with external form links and to report odd redirects.

Vendor engagement: security contact at vendor (security@forms-vendor.com) notified 2026-03-12 13:00 UTC; vendor confirmed third-party tracker inclusion and is investigating removal of query-string capture.

Permanent actions planned: update corporate egress policy to block common third-party form analytics domains by default, update CSP and Referrer-Policy guidance for vendor integrations, implement detection rule for URLs containing Okta/SSO token-like patterns in proxy logs, and add this scenario to security playbooks.
Post-incident: schedule a follow-up tabletop with Security, SRE, and Developer Relations to review vendor integration checklist and update procurement/security onboarding for third-party forms.
follow_up_tasks: TASK-1: Run search across proxy logs for any 'sid' tokens or JWT-like patterns in outbound requests in the last 30 days (owner: SRE, due: 2026-03-15)
TASK-2: Contact vendor for audit and proof of fix; obtain removal confirmation of third-party trackers (owner: Security Ops, due: 2026-03-16)
TASK-3: Add detection rule to SIEM for URL parameter patterns resembling Okta session tokens (owner: Detection Engineering, due: 2026-03-18)
TASK-4: Update vendor onboarding checklist to include a required security review of embedded analytics/referrers (owner: Developer Relations, due: 2026-03-25)

title: Possible SSO session replay after employee clicked external form link
preview:
Issue summary: An employee clicked a link in an externally hosted vendor form received via email. Within one hour, anomalous session activity for that account was observed: short-lived logins from unfamiliar IPs and requests that matched session-reuse patterns. Impact: potential session token exposure and attempted session replay. Initial scope: single user account (product-ops@redwood.ai), possibly more if the external form captured session info via query params or third-party trackers.

Timeline:
- 2026-03-12 09:27 UTC: Employee reports odd redirect after clicking vendor form link (Support Slack #sec-alerts).
- 2026-03-12 09:34 UTC: Proxy logs show outbound request to analytics.example-forms.com with a query string including a transient token-like value.
- 2026-03-12 09:45 UTC: Cloudflare auth logs show additional login events for product-ops@redwood.ai from two foreign IPs (AS12345) within a 10-minute window.
- 2026-03-12 10:10 UTC: Security detection flagged multiple short-lived sessions and created this ticket.

Environment: Production Redwood Console, us-east region, SSO via Okta (OIDC), corporate proxy (BlueGate) filtering egress.

Steps to reproduce (initial hypothesis):
1) User opens vendor email and clicks link to external hosted form (vendor domain: forms-vendor.com).
2) The hosted form includes third-party analytics (analytics.example-forms.com) which captures URL parameters.
3) A session identifier was appended to the redirect URL (possibly from the browser referrer or an incorrectly constructed omniauth return URL) and ended up in the analytics request.
4) Remote actor(s) replayed captured token, attempting to re-use the session against our auth endpoint.

Logs / evidence references:
- BlueGate proxy request: outbound GET to https://analytics.example-forms.com/collect?v=1&sid=eyJhbGci... (truncated) timestamp 2026-03-12T09:34:02Z (see proxy trace ID BG-8813).
- Cloudflare auth logs: login events for product-ops@redwood.ai from 185.62.44.10 and 185.62.44.11 at 09:45 UTC (location: offshore region); user agent: curl/7.85.0.
- Okta sessions API: session token for product-ops account was active from 09:25-09:48 UTC and then invalidated at 10:05 UTC.

Initial triage: Presumed session token leaked via URL parameter captured by third-party analytics. No evidence yet of privilege escalation or data exfil beyond attempted session reuse. We have temporarily revoked the user sessions and rotated API service tokens for the user's active ephemeral keys.

source.summary: Consolidate Q3 SaaS seat forecast and run security + procurement evaluation for new annotation vendor
source.description:
Issue summary:
- Request: Procure and onboard a small-pilot bundle of seats for a vendor evaluation (annotation tool) and reconcile Q3 seat forecast for Data Labeling and Research teams.
- Business impact: Labeling work for two product pilots (Search tuning and Safety evals) is blocked without a vetted annotation tool; delays will push model evaluation and release timelines by ~6 weeks.
- Requested scope: 25 initial editor seats + 5 manager seats (30 total) for 12 months (pilot -> expansion). Renewal cadence: annual.
- Vendor: LabelWave (SME annotation platform; SOC2 self-attested) — initial commercial quote attached in Confluence.
- Licensing model: per-seat annual subscription with optional storage add-on. Initial quoted cost: $36,000/yr (USD) for seats + $3,000 one-time migration assistance.
- Budget owner: ML Ops (cost center: ML-PLAT).
- Timeline: Need pilot access by 2026-04-01 to support labeling sprints starting Week 1 April.
- Security requirements: SOC2 Type II or equivalent evidence, vendor risk questionnaire, Data Processing Agreement (DPA), encryption at rest, and support for Okta SSO + SCIM provisioning. No PHI expected; data will include ML training examples and internal pseudonymized user content.
- Operational items: provisioning via SCIM, role mappings (editor/manager), audit log forwarding (if available), and retention policy alignment with Redwood retention standards.
- Evaluation criteria: security checklist pass, successful SCIM provisioning in staging, labeler throughput metrics (annotations/user/hour), and cost per label estimates compared to current ad-hoc tooling.
- Acceptance criteria: Security completes vendor assessment and marks as approved OR provides a list of mitigating controls; Procurement executes pilot contract; Identity provisions SCIM + Okta SSO; Finance confirms budget allocation.
- Attachments / links: Confluence vendor comparison + quote (see related_confluence_pages).

Steps to onboard / proposed playbook:
1) Procurement: confirm vendor quote, negotiate pilot term (3 months trial or credit), and draft PO.
2) Security: run vendor questionnaire (ask for SOC2 report, pen-test results, any subprocessor list).
3) Identity: validate SCIM endpoints and create test Okta app in staging.
4) Ops/ML: prepare sample dataset, test import/export, and verify retention controls.
5) Finance: pre-approve $36k headroom and route cost center charge.
6) Pilot kickoff: create 10 pilot seats, evaluate for 4 weeks, then expand to full 30 seats if OK.

Notes / risks:
- If LabelWave cannot provide SOC2 evidence, Security may require extra contractual safeguards or deny vendor onboarding.
- SCIM gaps will add manual provisioning overhead and may push timeline beyond target.
- Contractual minimums could negate the value of a small pilot; negotiate a flexible pilot credit.

Relevant historical/context links:
- See INT-142506 for precedent onboarding Doccano (similar annotation procurement): https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INT-142506
comments: 2026-03-09 09:12 - Ava Chen (reporter): Filing this to consolidate seat forecast for Q3 and request a short pilot purchase. Added initial numbers from ML Ops planning doc.
2026-03-09 10:45 - Procurement (Elena Rivera): Acknowledged. Will request an updated quote and confirm pilot pricing. ETA 2026-03-12.
2026-03-10 14:02 - Security (Priya Nair): Vendor questionnaire sent to LabelWave contact. Requested SOC2 Type II and a list of subprocessors. Expect first results by 2026-03-15.
2026-03-11 08:33 - Identity (Samir Gupta): Can validate SCIM + Okta in staging. Need a test account and staging API endpoints from LabelWave. Tagging on-call SRE for staging network access if needed.
2026-03-12 16:20 - Finance (Noah Rivera): Marked as P2; finance can provision approval for up to $40k pending final contract terms and PO. Will require GL code and cost center (ML-PLAT).

2026-03-13 11:07 - Procurement (Elena Rivera): Vendor provided revised pilot quote with a 3-month credit and an option to scale seats monthly. Draft PO prepared and uploaded to Confluence (link in related_confluence_pages).
2026-03-13 15:44 - Marcus Patel (assignee): Scheduling a sync with ML Ops and Security next week to review questionnaire results and confirm go/no-go for pilot onboarding.
source.notes: Procurement to negotiate pilot flexibility; Security to prioritize SOC2 evidence. Target pilot start 2026-04-01 if no blockers.

source.summary: Okta SSO login triggers captive-portal detection which drops VPN and causes authentication loop for remote Wi‑Fi users
source.description:
Issue summary: Several employees connecting from customer sites or hotel Wi‑Fi report that attempting to authenticate to internal apps via Okta SAML redirects triggers a captive‑portal flow. When users complete captive‑portal sign‑in, the VPN client (WireGuard-based corporate agent) detects the network change and tears down the tunnel, which invalidates the SSO session and returns the user to the captive portal — creating a loop.

Impact: Multiple remote employees (mostly traveling sales and solutions engineers) are unable to access internal dashboards or Atlassian tools until they fully disconnect and re‑authenticate. Affected users temporarily lose access to internal services for ~10–30 minutes. No customer‑facing systems impacted; internal productivity degradation.

Environment: macOS and Windows corporate images, Redwood VPN agent v2.9.1 (WireGuard wrapper), Okta SAML SSO, typical captive portal networks (hotel, conference Wi‑Fi), region: global (most reports from APAC/EMEA travel).

Observed behavior: SSO redirect to okta.company.com triggers captive‑portal HTTP/302 to the provider portal; the VPN agent sees an interface change and initiates rekey/reenroll which tears down existing sockets. Okta session cookie becomes invalid after the rebind and the user is presented with the captive portal again.

Expected behavior: Completing captive‑portal flow should retain network connection and VPN agent should gracefully wait for interface stabilization or pause rekey until user login completes so SSO exchange can finish.

Temporary workaround: Users must disconnect VPN, complete captive portal authentication, then re‑start VPN and re‑login to Okta. Recommended to perform captive‑portal sign‑in in a browser before starting VPN where possible.
steps_to_reproduce: Connect laptop to a hotel or conference Wi‑Fi that exposes a captive portal
Start Redwood corporate VPN agent (v2.9.1) and establish tunnel to prod edge gateway
Open browser and navigate to an internal application that redirects to Okta SAML login
Observe captive portal intercepting the Okta redirect and showing login/terms page
Complete captive portal flow — VPN agent detects network change and rebinds interface
After rebind, Okta session fails to complete and browser is redirected back to captive portal — loop observed
logs: Mar 10 08:12:23 laptop01 redwood-vpn[924]: wg0: rebind triggered due to default route change (ifindex 5 -> 12)
Mar 10 08:12:24 laptop01 redwood-vpn[924]: wg0: handshakes reset, closing sockets
Mar 10 08:12:26 laptop01 chrome[1129]: GET https://okta.company.com/sso/saml 302 -> http://captive.portal.local/portal
Mar 10 08:12:30 laptop01 redwood-vpn[924]: vpn-control: attempting reconnect (session invalidated)
Client-side: browser shows captive portal form; filling form returns 200 but Okta SAML state lost after VPN reconnect
investigation_notes: 03/10 09:00 — Reproduced locally using hotspot with captive portal emulator. Confirmed rebind leads to wg handshake reset and Okta SAML state token invalidation.
03/10 10:15 — Hypothesis A: VPN agent aggressively tears down on route change instead of waiting for netstack stabilization; this invalidates ephemeral SSO state tied to source IP/port.
03/11 11:40 — Hypothesis B: Edge gateway validates client IP for SAML callback and rejects requests after a short rekey window; check edge ID binding logic.
03/11 14:05 — Captured packet trace during reproduce; SAML POST arrives from new ephemeral port/IP after rebind and is rejected by Okta due to state mismatch.
03/12 09:30 — Tested with VPN agent flag --delay-on-network-change=5s (experimental) — reduced occurrence but still racey at high-latency captive portals.
Relevant config: wg persistent-keepalive 25s; agent performs immediate handshake tear-down on ifindex change.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:12 — Sofia Martinez: Initial report from three travelers (EMEA sales). Attached client logs and steps. Marking P1 due to multiple people blocked.
2026-03-10 10:00 — Ethan Park: I can reproduce locally. Captive portal causes the agent to call 'rebind_if_changed' which resets handshake. Adding packet captures to investigation_notes.
2026-03-11 14:30 — Aaron Li (SRE): Edge teams — can you confirm whether callbacks are validated by source IP? If yes, we need to allow short ephemeral callback windows for SAML flows.
2026-03-11 15:05 — Priya Nair (Edge): Edge currently has strict source IP mapping for session state. We can add temporary relaxation for SAML callback endpoints; proposing feature flag rollout.
2026-03-12 09:45 — Ethan Park: Verified that adding 5s debounce in agent reduces failures in moderate networks. Need product decision: ship debounce vs. edge relaxation vs. Okta change.
2026-03-13 08:20 — Security: Concern noted about relaxing source-IP checks for SAML. We need telemetry and a limited flag for only SAML endpoints and maintain audit logs.
2026-03-13 12:00 — Ethan Park: Creating PR to add --delay-on-network-change configurable flag and edge change proposal. Will coordinate canary with SRE and Security.
source.resolution: Under investigation — proposed mitigation: ship a short debounce (configurable) in VPN agent to wait for network stabilization before tearing down handshakes; simultaneously propose a narrow edge relaxation feature-flag for SAML callback endpoints. Security review required.

source.summary: Device-trust approval delay triggers repeated console access-request loop
source.description:
Issue summary:
A subset of internal users attempting to sign in to the Redwood Console are stuck in an access-request loop: the console shows "Waiting for device approval" and the platform repeatedly creates new pending access-request records instead of updating the existing request. Approvers are not receiving stable notifications, and on-call pages are being generated for duplicate requests.

Impact:
- ~12 engineers across two squads reported inability to complete console login via SSO (device approval required).
- Flood of duplicate access-request tickets in the admin queue causing approver overload and delayed approvals.
- Temporary manual approvals required for critical oncall responders. No production compute or model serving impact observed.

Environment:
- Prod sign-in flow through Okta + Device Trust + internal SSO bridge (sso-bridge). Region: us-west. Affected users appear to be on iOS-managed devices and a few macOS devices with the latest MDM profile.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1. User attempts console sign-in via Okta SSO from managed device.
2. Okta returns device-challenge required -> device-trust-service triggers an approval request to the approver queue.
3. The user approves on-device; device-trust service signals success, but sso-bridge polls the approval endpoint and receives intermittent 202 / empty payload due to a race with the approval indexer.
4. sso-bridge treats the response as "no approved record" and re-creates a new access-request entry (new request_id), producing duplicate pending items.
5. Approver webhooks and email notifications are rate-limited and deduplicated imperfectly, so notifications are inconsistent.

Observed logs (snippets):
- sso-bridge.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:03Z] warn: approval-poll no-record for device-req token=abc123 polling=3"
- device-trust-service.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:02Z] info: approval indexer wrote request_id=R-20260311-7"
- auth-proxy.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:04Z] error: duplicate-access-request created new id R-20260311-8 for user=j.lee@example.com"

Hypothesis:
- A race condition between the approval indexer and the sso-bridge polling leads to a transient 202/no-body response that sso-bridge treats as "not approved" and creates a new request instead of waiting/backing off.
- Notification/dedup logic in admin-queue assumes stable request IDs and does not correlate repeated requests for the same user/device.

Immediate mitigation (already applied):
- Manual approval of critical oncall requests by SRE to restore login access.
- Temporarily increased visibility: added a dashboard query to show access-request creation rate and duplicate_count per user.

Proposed fixes / next steps:
- Short-term (hotfix): Update sso-bridge polling to interpret 202/no-body as a retryable transient and implement exponential backoff + idempotency key lookup before creating a new request. (PR planned).
- Medium-term: Harden approval indexer to emit a pre-write lease or a transient marker so pollers can detect in-progress writes.
- Long-term: Improve admin notification deduplication by matching on user+device fingerprint and recent requests (rolling window).

Attachments / references:
- Slack thread: #support (https://redwood.slack.com/archives/C0123456)
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.internal/display/OPS/Device+Trust+Runbook
comments: 2026-03-10 15:18 - Jordan Blake (reporter): Users from Platform infra and Search squad reported repeated 'Waiting for device approval' after approving on-device. I reproduced with my MDM-managed phone. Created this ticket to track and escalate.
2026-03-10 16:05 - Oncall (SRE): Temporarily approved affected users manually. Noted 4 duplicate pending items for same user in admin queue. Investigating logs now.

2026-03-11 09:12 - Riley Chen (assignee): Found a pattern in sso-bridge: polling loop treats 202 with empty body as 'no approval' and creates a new request. Capture attached logs. Will attempt a non-prod repro by simulating indexer lag.

2026-03-11 11:30 - Eng (device-trust): Confirmed indexer writes can be delayed by up to 600ms under heavy load; we currently do not expose a 'write-in-progress' marker. Idea: emit a temp-token header to signal in-progress write.

2026-03-12 08:47 - Riley Chen: Rolled a quick config change in sso-bridge in staging to add 250ms initial backoff before creating a new request; observed reduced duplicates in smoke tests. Preparing PR for prod hotfix with idempotency lookup.

2026-03-13 10:02 - Product Security: Flagged as potential user-impact/security observation because access-requests could be both duplicated and occasionally misattributed; recommended we audit the admin queue for orphaned requests older than 48h.
2026-03-13 14:00 - Riley Chen: Created PR #12567 to add idempotency-key check and backoff. Linked below. Scheduling quick rollbackable deploy to canary in us-west tomorrow morning.
source.sre_workaround: Manual approvals by SRE for critical oncall users; added dashboard to track duplicates; temporary staging config added for backoff testing.
follow_up_tasks: Add write-in-progress marker to approval indexer
Audit admin queue for orphaned requests >48h

- Temporary manual approvals required for critical oncall responders. No production compute or model serving impact observed.

Environment:
- Prod sign-in flow through Okta + Device Trust + internal SSO bridge (sso-bridge). Region: us-west. Affected users appear to be on iOS-managed devices and a few macOS devices with the latest MDM profile.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1. User attempts console sign-in via Okta SSO from managed device.
2. Okta returns device-challenge required -> device-trust-service triggers an approval request to the approver queue.
3. The user approves on-device; device-trust service signals success, but sso-bridge polls the approval endpoint and receives intermittent 202 / empty payload due to a race with the approval indexer.
4. sso-bridge treats the response as "no approved record" and re-creates a new access-request entry (new request_id), producing duplicate pending items.
5. Approver webhooks and email notifications are rate-limited and deduplicated imperfectly, so notifications are inconsistent.

Observed logs (snippets):
- sso-bridge.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:03Z] warn: approval-poll no-record for device-req token=abc123 polling=3"
- device-trust-service.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:02Z] info: approval indexer wrote request_id=R-20260311-7"
- auth-proxy.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:04Z] error: duplicate-access-request created new id R-20260311-8 for user=j.lee@example.com"

Hypothesis:
- A race condition between the approval indexer and the sso-bridge polling leads to a transient 202/no-body response that sso-bridge treats as "not approved" and creates a new request instead of waiting/backing off.
- Notification/dedup logic in admin-queue assumes stable request IDs and does not correlate repeated requests for the same user/device.

Immediate mitigation (already applied):
- Manual approval of critical oncall requests by SRE to restore login access.
- Temporarily increased visibility: added a dashboard query to show access-request creation rate and duplicate_count per user.

Proposed fixes / next steps:
- Short-term (hotfix): Update sso-bridge polling to interpret 202/no-body as a retryable transient and implement exponential backoff + idempotency key lookup before creating a new request. (PR planned).
- Medium-term: Harden approval indexer to emit a pre-write lease or a transient marker so pollers can detect in-progress writes.
- Long-term: Improve admin notification deduplication by matching on user+device fingerprint and recent requests (rolling window).

Attachments / references:
- Slack thread: #support (https://redwood.slack.com/archives/C0123456)
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.internal/display/OPS/Device+Trust+Runbook

source.summary: Session linkage token mismatch blocks cross-account impersonation for support tooling
source.description:
Issue summary: Since 2026-03-12 09:10 UTC, support tooling (impersonation flows used by oncall engineers via the internal console and CLI) intermittently fail with a 'session token mismatch' error when initiating cross-account impersonation. Impact: Oncall engineers cannot assume customer-facing service accounts for 5-30% of impersonation attempts, blocking urgent runbook actions and escalations. Environment: production us-east, identity-proxy v3.4.1, okta-device-trust enabled for privileged flows.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Login to console as admin (device trust approved)
2) Open 'Switch account / Impersonate' dialog and select external-tenant service-account
3) Click 'Assume role', or run 'rwd auth impersonate --account <tenant-id>' via CLI
4) Observe immediate failure in UI or CLI with error code 403/SESSION_LINK_MISMATCH and short correlation id in logs

Observed behavior: The identity-proxy returns 403 with message 'session_linkage_token invalid for target session'. The console shows a transient toast 'Unable to start impersonation — session verification failed'. CLI returns non-zero exit and logs: 'auth: session_linkage: verification failed: token not in session map'.

Expected behavior: Token exchange should validate and produce a scoped session for the target account or fall back to a clear diagnostic flow requesting reauthorization.

Investigation notes:
- Correlation IDs collected from affected attempts: rwd-imp-3f9b, rwd-imp-3fa0, rwd-cli-4b12. Logs attached to ticket in the support bucket (s3://redwood-support/logs/impersonation/2026-03-12/).
- Identity-proxy logs show the incoming assertion includes a linkage_token header (base64 string). The session-store (redis cluster auth-sessions-prod) lookup for the linkage token intermittently returns a 5xx from the replica nodes leading to token not found. The failure window aligns with a 2-minute rolling restart of redis-read replicas (maintenance job kicked off at 09:09 UTC by infra auto-heal).
- Reproduced locally by toggling a TTL skew: if session-store read misses then the verification fails immediately with the same error.
- No Okta assertion failures observed; device-trust asserts ok.
- Related code paths: identity-proxy handler verifySessionLinkage(), session-cache middleware, rwd-auth-cli token exchange.

Temporary mitigation:
- Oncall instructed to retry impersonation (most retries succeed). A short-term runbook added to the support-confluence page.
- Infra team paused the rolling restart and warmed read replicas; observed reduction in failures.

Next steps / Proposed fix:
1) Add a robust retry layer to session-store reads in identity-proxy (backoff + failover to primary) — PR proposed: https://github.com/redwood-inference/identity-proxy/pull/1872
2) Improve observability: emit metrics for session-linkage cache misses and 5xx on redis replicas. Dashboard ticket created under MON-impersonation-calls.
3) Add defensive behavior: if linkage token not found but assertion from Okta valid, present clear UI guidance to reauthorize rather than generic 403.

References: Slack thread #support-impersonation (https://slack.redwoodinference/support-impersonation/p12345), PR above, redis maintenance logs (infra ticket INT-931200).
comments: 2026-03-12 10:05 UTC — Ethan Park: Picked up for triage. Confirmed correlation ids and logs. Reached out to infra to pause replica restart.
2026-03-12 10:22 UTC — Infra (L1): Paused rolling restart. Observed reduced errors for 20m. We are investigating replica health and failover policy.
2026-03-12 11:03 UTC — Marisol Vega: Customer-impacting oncall action blocked for a span. Documented interim retry guidance in runbook. Added troubleshooting checklist.

2026-03-12 13:47 UTC — SRE (Arjun Mehta): Root cause likely race between replica restart and streaming session replication. Working on a fix path: ensure reads fallback to primary on replica errors. Will prepare a hotfix to identity-proxy.

2026-03-13 09:12 UTC — Ethan Park: Created PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/identity-proxy/pull/1872 with retry/backoff on session-store reads. Ran canary in staging; passed synthetic impersonation tests.

2026-03-13 16:30 UTC — Release (Nora Liu): Rolled identity-proxy hotfix to prod-us-east with gradual rollout. Monitoring dashboard shows linkage failure rate down to baseline. Marking ticket for validation before close.

source.summary: Okta MFA enrollment loop prevents SSO access to internal Console for new hires
source.description:
Issue summary:
Several new hires cannot complete Okta SSO to access the Redwood Console because they enter an MFA enrollment loop. The Okta flow repeatedly prompts for MFA enrollment and then immediately returns to the login screen without granting a session. This affects onboarding engineers and blocks critical access to runbooks and incident tooling.

Impact:
- 8 new employees (onboarding cohort) unable to access console since 2026-03-12 08:20 UTC.
- Prevents completion of onboarding tasks and emergency incident response for affected users.

Environment:
- Redwood Console (https://console.redwood.ai) behind Okta SSO.
- Affected in prod/us-east for new users created through SCIM this morning.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create test user via SCIM with same group set as onboarding cohort.
2) Attempt first-time SSO login at https://console.redwood.ai via Okta.
3) Okta prompts for MFA enrollment (SMS / Authenticator).
4) After selecting a factor and completing enrollment flow, Okta redirects back to login and immediately prompts for MFA enrollment again (loop).

Observed behavior / logs:
- Okta auth logs (excerpt):
  - user.login.attempt produced eventId=0oab1234xyz; outcome=FAILURE; reason=FACTOR_ENROLLMENT_INCOMPLETE
  - factor.enroll.attempt eventId=0ocd5678abc; outcome=SUCCESS; enrollmentState=ENROLLMENT_BLOCKED
- Browser network: repeated POST /idp/idx/interaction/2003 responses returning remediation requiring enroll factor again.
- Console shows 401 for session validation when Okta returns session token.

First response SLA due: 2026-03-12
steps_to_reproduce: Create user via SCIM with onboarding groups
Attempt SSO first-time login to console
Complete MFA enrollment when prompted
Observe immediate re-prompt for enrollment and no session created
logs: 2026-03-12T08:21:03Z okta.auth - eventId=0oab1234xyz outcome=FAILURE reason=FACTOR_ENROLLMENT_INCOMPLETE
2026-03-12T08:21:10Z okta.factor - eventId=0ocd5678abc outcome=SUCCESS enrollmentState=ENROLLMENT_BLOCKED
Browser POST /idp/idx/interaction/2003 -> remediation: enroll-factor
comments: Ava Chen - 2026-03-12: Reporting initial impact after onboarding cohort reported inability to access console. Attached OKTA auth log excerpts and sample user IDs.
Diego Morales - 2026-03-12: Assigned. Reproduced with a SCIM-provisioned test user. Adding Priya (SRE) for token/session validation checks. Creating a temp Slack thread #support-auth-9512.

Priya Patel - 2026-03-12: Observed Okta returns a session token but our console session validation rejects with 401. Hypothesis: new Okta policy change introduced device registration or custom enrollment action that sets enrollmentState to BLOCKED until additional profile criteria are met.

Diego Morales - 2026-03-12: Cross-referencing with recent infra change: GitHub PR org/infra/okta-policy#548 modified MFA rules for group 'onboarding-engineers' earlier today. Adding linked issue INT-9470 for rollback request.
Sanjay Rao (IAM) - 2026-03-12: Confirmed policy change. Will hold on further changes until resolution. Provided short-term emergency bypass codes to affected users.
Ava Chen - 2026-03-13: Two new hires were able to access console after using bypass codes. Requesting permanent fix and runbook updates.

Diego Morales - 2026-03-13: Policy rollback applied in Okta at 2026-03-13T02:14Z. Confirmed successful first-time SSO for recreated test user. Drafting follow-up PR to refine policy and add automated alerts.
investigation_notes: Reproduced using SCIM test account and dev browser session. Okta log shows enrollmentState=ENROLLMENT_BLOCKED immediately after factor enrollment success.

Reviewed GitHub PR org/infra/okta-policy#548 - change added 'requireDeviceRegistration' flag for group 'onboarding-engineers' — likely causing additional server-side checks that our console isn't surfacing.
Session validation flow: Okta issues sessionToken -> Console exchanges via /api/auth/sso -> our session creation returns 401 when Okta 'enrollment' claim exists but 'deviceRegistered' claim absent.
No prior alerts fired for policy changes. Need to wire Okta system logs into config-change alerting.
source.resolution:
Temporary workaround: issued emergency bypass codes and advised affected users to use them to complete initial login. Permanent fix: rolled back the requireDeviceRegistration flag in Okta policy for 'onboarding-engineers' at 2026-03-13T02:14Z which restored normal MFA enrollment flow. Verified by creating fresh SCIM user and completing SSO without loop.

Follow-ups (tracked as INT-9513): update Okta policy change process to include automated alerts, add a step in onboarding runbook for emergency bypass procedure, and add a console-side check to surface Okta enrollmentState metadata for clearer user messaging.
follow_up_tasks: INT-9513 - Add automated alerts for Okta policy changes
INT-9520 - Update onboarding runbook with MFA bypass procedure
INT-9531 - Console: surface Okta enrollmentState in login error UX
source.notes_for_docs: Add FAQ entry for onboarding MFA enrollment loop, include steps for emergency bypass and contact points (#support, IAM).

source.summary: Service accounts lost Console/CI access after Okta group prefix remapping
source.description: Issue summary: Several internal service accounts and a small set of CI pipelines started receiving 403/permission-denied errors when calling the Redwood Console API and internal admin endpoints immediately after an Okta group naming convention change was applied by Identity. Impact: 7 service accounts and 4 CI workflows failed auth checks; internal tooling and alerting UIs were inaccessible for owners. Environment: Production identity sync (SCIM) + Redwood Console RBAC mapping, us-east region. Timeline: group prefix remapping applied 2026-03-10 07:15 UTC, first failures observed 07:21 UTC. Expected: group membership mapping should preserve effective RBAC roles for service principals and bot users. Observed: group-based roles no longer resolved for SAML/SCIM-provisioned service accounts because Okta attribute used for mapping changed from groups.name to groups.displayName in the recent change.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) In Okta admin, rename group prefix from 'redwood-' to 'rw-'.
2) Trigger SCIM sync (manual or scheduled).
3) Attempt to authenticate as a service account that previously relied on the 'redwood-*' group membership to receive the 'infra-admin' RBAC role in Console.
4) Observe 403 responses from Console API and failed CI job steps that call internal admin APIs.
logs: Console API error example: 2026-03-10T07:22:03Z GET /api/admin/teams 403 {"error":"forbidden","message":"not authorized"}
Okta SCIM sync: 2026-03-10T07:16:29Z sync run completed; 12 groups updated; mapped attribute: groups.displayName
CI job log: 2026-03-10T07:23:11Z step 'deploy-migrations' failed with exit 1: permission denied (403) when calling /api/admin/migrate
investigation_notes: Initial triage by SRE (Maya) confirmed failures coincide with Identity team change window (Slack #it-support thread).
Checked SCIM mappings: application connector configured to map Okta 'groups' attribute. Recent Okta change changed the attribute payload to include displayName but not name for renamed groups.
Hypothesis: RBAC lookup on Console side matches Okta group's 'name' field; when Rename/prefix change occurred and SCIM began sending displayName instead, group-to-role mapping failed.
Queried user-service: service account entries still reference old group ids, but Okta membership shows new group id/name mapping. No explicit deprovisioning occurred.

Relevant links: Slack #it-support thread (https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C0123ABC), infra PR that altered SCIM connector (github.com/redwood-infra/okta-sync/commit/3a9b7f), Confluence runbook for Okta group mappings.
comments: 2026-03-10 08:05 - Maya Chen: Confirmed 7 service accounts lost effective 'infra-admin' binding. Collecting list and owners.

2026-03-10 09:12 - Aaron Patel (Identity): We rolled a change to group attribute mapping to 'displayName' to support new naming policy. Didn't realize Console expects 'name'. Rolling back now and re-running SCIM sync.
2026-03-10 09:25 - Maya Chen: Rollback initiated; created temporary elevated RBAC bindings for affected service accounts to unblock CI. Will monitor for recovery post-sync.
2026-03-11 10:40 - Ethan Park: CI owners confirmed jobs passed after rollback and re-sync. Marking as under verification.
resolution_notes: Cause: Okta group attribute mapping changed (groups.displayName used) and Console RBAC engine matched groups by 'name'; mismatch caused role resolution to fail.

Short-term fix: Identity rolled back the attribute mapping to groups.name and re-triggered SCIM sync at 2026-03-10T09:18 UTC. SRE applied temporary direct RBAC grants to 7 affected service accounts to restore CI workflows during remediation.

Long-term fix: Updated Console RBAC matcher to accept both groups.name and groups.displayName (PR: github.com/redwood-infra/console/pull/901) and added a schema validation check for incoming SCIM payloads. Also filed INT-13035 to run an audit of all group-based bindings and to add alerting when SCIM payload schema changes.
Follow-up: Identity to notify IT before group naming policy changes; add a pre-change runbook and a test sync in staging. Confluence page updated with postmortem notes.
Resolved for now 2026-03-11; monitoring window until 2026-03-15.
